proposals.title: Engineering High-Performance Mixed-Matrix Membranes from Ultra-Rigid Glassy Polymers and Metal-Organic Frameworks for Challenging Gas Separations
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) traditionally rely on flexible, rubbery polymers or moderately rigid glassy polymers to ensure good interfacial adhesion and defect-free fabrication. This limits the use of ultra-high-performance, rigid glassy polymers (e.g., polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs), thermally rearranged (TR) polymers, certain polyimides) which offer exceptional intrinsic selectivity but are brittle and difficult to process. Overcoming this limitation would unlock a new class of MMMs with unprecedented separation performance for energy-intensive gas pairs like O2/N2, CO2/CH4, and olefin/paraffin separations.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing a tailored, multi-step physical aging and annealing protocol, combined with judicious selection of surface-functionalized, rigid MOF fillers (e.g., ZIF-8, UiO-66), it is possible to fabricate defect-free, high-loading MMMs from exclusively rigid, high-Tg glassy polymers, thereby decoupling membrane performance from polymer chain flexibility.
proposals.method: We will synthesize asymmetric hollow fiber membranes from a high-performance, rigid glassy polymer (e.g., a PIM-1 or a high-free-volume polyimide). A series of MMMs will be fabricated by incorporating MOF nanoparticles (20-40 wt%) pre-treated with vinyltrimethoxysilane or similar agents to enhance interfacial compatibility. The critical step involves a controlled, multi-stage thermal treatment: initial low-temperature annealing below Tg to relieve casting stresses, followed by a precise above-Tg annealing step (inspired by Koros's recent work on CMS membranes) to promote polymer chain relaxation and interfacial healing around the rigid MOF particles, before a final rapid quench to lock in the optimized microstructure. Performance will be evaluated via high-pressure mixed-gas permeation tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel fabrication paradigm for MMMs, expanding the usable polymer matrix library to the most selective but process-challenging materials. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of thermal history and interfacial engineering in rigid-rigid composite systems. The resulting membranes are expected to demonstrate a simultaneous enhancement in selectivity and permeability, breaking conventional trade-off limits for key gas pairs.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the prevailing requirement for polymer flexibility in MMM fabrication. The novelty lies in intentionally using exclusively rigid components and leveraging controlled physical aging and above-Tg annealing—techniques recently advanced for CMS membranes—to manage interfacial stresses and defects in a wholly glassy composite system.

proposals.title: Controlled Polymer Grafting from MOF Surfaces via Surface-Initiated ATRP for Enhanced Interface Stability in Mixed-Matrix Membranes
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) incorporating metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offer great potential for advanced gas separations, but their performance is often limited by weak organic-inorganic interfaces, leading to non-selective voids and physical aging. Pre-2026, strategies to improve MOF-polymer compatibility, such as silane treatment, are common but can be limited in creating robust, covalently bonded interfaces. A more fundamental approach to engineer the interface is needed to unlock the full performance and stability of MOF-based MMMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that covalently grafting polymer chains directly from MOF surfaces via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) will create a mechanically interlocked and chemically bonded interface, significantly reducing interfacial defects and suppressing physical aging in the resulting MMMs.
proposals.method: We will functionalize the external surfaces of ZIF-8 and UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles with ATRP initiators. Using a 'grafting-from' approach, we will grow poly(methyl methacrylate) and poly(1-trimethylsilyl-1-propyne) chains of controlled length and density directly from the MOF surfaces via SI-ATRP. The core-shell MOF@polymer particles will be characterized (TGA, FTIR, TEM) and incorporated into high-performance polymers like 6FDA-DAM to form asymmetric MMMs. Membrane performance (CO2/CH4, O2/N2 separation) and long-term stability under aggressive aging conditions (elevated temperature/pressure) will be rigorously evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, covalent method to engineer the MOF-polymer interface, moving beyond simple surface modification. It will provide fundamental insights into how grafted polymer architecture (length, density) dictates MMM morphology, transport, and stability, contributing a new materials platform for durable, high-flux membranes.
proposals.novelty: While polymer grafting to flat inorganic surfaces is known, its systematic application to porous MOFs for MMMs, combined with Koros's expertise in physical aging and asymmetric membrane formation, creates a novel pathway to decouple filler dispersion from interface quality and achieve unprecedented long-term performance.

proposals.title: Polymer-Templated Growth of Metal-Organic Frameworks for Tailored Microstructure and Enhanced Gas Separation Performance
proposals.motivation: The performance of metal-organic framework (MOF)-based membranes is critically dependent on controlling crystal size, orientation, and defect structure. Conventional in-situ growth methods often yield poorly intergrown or randomly oriented crystals, limiting selectivity and mechanical stability. Using polymers as dynamic templates offers a promising route to direct MOF nucleation and growth, potentially overcoming these integration challenges for next-generation hybrid membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: That flexible, functional polymers containing coordinating groups (e.g., polyimides with carboxylic acid side chains) can modulate the local chemical environment during solvothermal synthesis to template the growth of ZIF-8 and UiO-66 crystals, leading to controlled crystal size, preferred orientation, and improved adhesion within an asymmetric polymer matrix.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of functional polyimides with varying chain flexibility and densities of pendant carboxylic acid groups. These polymers will be used to fabricate asymmetric hollow fiber supports via phase inversion. The fibers will then be immersed in MOF precursor solutions for solvothermal growth. The influence of polymer chemistry, annealing history (inspired by 'above-Tg' stabilization), and synthesis conditions on MOF crystal size, morphology, and membrane microstructure will be characterized using SEM, XRD, and XPS. Gas permeation (CO2/CH4, O2/N2) and physical aging studies will evaluate separation performance and stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental understanding of polymer-MOF interfacial interactions during crystal growth, moving beyond simple blending or seeding. It will provide design rules for creating hybrid membranes with optimized microstructure for gas separation, directly contributing to the development of more efficient carbon capture and natural gas purification processes.
proposals.novelty: While polymer-MOF mixed matrix membranes are common, using the polymer support as an active template to *direct* crystal growth kinetics and orientation, rather than as a passive host, is a non-obvious strategy. This approach synergizes Koros's expertise in asymmetric polymer membranes and physical aging with the templating concept to achieve unprecedented microstructural control.

proposals.title: Engineering Asymmetric MOF-Polymer Mixed Matrix Membranes for High-Performance, Aging-Resistant Organic Solvent Nanofiltration
proposals.motivation: Liquid-phase separations, such as organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN), are critical for energy-efficient chemical and pharmaceutical manufacturing, but face challenges in achieving high selectivity and solvent stability. Mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) have revolutionized gas separations, yet their application in demanding liquid environments remains limited due to issues like filler-polymer incompatibility, swelling-induced aging, and poor performance under chemical potential gradients. Advancing MMMs for liquids would unlock new purification modalities for complex solvent streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating surface-modified, nanoporous MOFs (e.g., ZIF-8, UiO-66) into an asymmetric, high-performance polymer matrix (e.g., Matrimid® or a polyimide) via a tailored phase-inversion process will yield MMMs with superior and stable liquid-phase separation performance by creating synergistic, size-selective pathways that resist physical aging and plasticization.
proposals.method: The approach involves: 1) Synthesizing and functionalizing MOF nanoparticles (e.g., with vinyltrimethoxysilane analogs) to enhance polymer-MOF interface compatibility. 2) Fabricating asymmetric flat-sheet MMMs via a non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS) process, leveraging Koros's expertise in asymmetric membrane formation, to create a thin, selective skin layer. 3) Systematically evaluating membrane performance for OSN (e.g., dye/solvent or pharmaceutical isomer separations), measuring flux and rejection under realistic chemical potential gradients. 4) Characterizing physical aging and stability via long-term solvent exposure tests and probing interfacial morphology using advanced microscopy and positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework for designing robust MOF-polymer MMMs specifically for liquid separations, directly extending Koros's gas-phase MMM and aging research. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of asymmetric morphology and interfacial engineering on long-term performance under solvent stress, contributing new structure-property relationships for the membrane community.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted adaptation of gas-separation MMM design principles—focusing on interface control and asymmetric structure—to solve distinct challenges in liquid-phase operations (swelling, aging in solvents), moving beyond the mere incorporation of MOFs into dense films to create integral asymmetric MMMs for OSN.

proposals.title: High-Flux, High-Selectivity MOF Membranes via Solvent-Resistant, Non-Polymeric Macroporous Supports Fabricated by Phase Inversion Sintering
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic framework (MOF) membranes offer exceptional separation potential but require robust, high-surface-area supports for thin, defect-free layer formation. Traditional asymmetric polymeric supports, while effective for many gas separations, can suffer from plasticization, compaction, and instability in aggressive organic solvent or high-pressure environments, limiting their application scope. A non-polymeric, macroporous support with tailored surface morphology could enable the deployment of MOF membranes in challenging chemical separations where polymer supports fail.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a macroporous, solvent-resistant ceramic or carbon support, engineered with a graded pore structure and surface chemistry via phase inversion and sintering of particle/polymer blends, will provide an ideal platform for growing high-performance, thin MOF layers, leading to membranes with superior stability and separation performance in organic solvent reverse osmosis (OSRO) or high-pressure gas separations.
proposals.method: We will fabricate supports using a phase-inversion process with blends of inorganic particles (e.g., alumina, silicon carbide) or carbon precursors with a sacrificial polymer binder, followed by controlled sintering to create an asymmetric, macroporous structure. The support surface will be modified using techniques like silanization (inspired by Koros's work on SBA-15) to introduce functional groups for enhancing MOF nucleation. Thin, continuous layers of high-performance MOFs (e.g., ZIF-8, UiO-66) will be deposited via contra-diffusion or secondary growth methods. Membrane performance will be evaluated for separations like xylene isomers or propylene/propane, with stability assessed under aggressive solvents and high-pressure aging conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel membrane architecture that decouples the demanding mechanical/chemical stability of the support from the selective layer, extending the operational window of MOF membranes. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay between non-polymeric support morphology/surface chemistry and the growth of high-quality MOF films. The resulting membranes are expected to demonstrate unprecedented stability in harsh conditions, advancing molecular separation technologies for the petrochemical and pharmaceutical industries.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the phase-inversion and sintering concept—typically for creating porous ceramic structures—specifically to engineer a support for MOF membranes, moving beyond polymeric or dense ceramic discs. This approach directly targets the stability limitations of polymer supports in Koros's recent work on solvent purification and aging, creating a new platform for his group's expertise in performance optimization and physical aging studies.

proposals.title: Controlling Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) Deposition via Solvent-Pair Selection and Flow Dynamics for High-Performance Asymmetric Mixed-Matrix Membranes
proposals.motivation: Incorporating MOFs into polymer matrices to create mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) is a leading strategy to transcend the polymer performance upper bound for gas separations. However, achieving a defect-free, high-quality MOF-polymer interface, especially in asymmetric membranes, remains a critical challenge. The deposition process, influenced by solvent interactions and flow dynamics during fabrication, directly dictates MOF dispersion, adhesion, and ultimately membrane selectivity and permeability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically pairing solvents with specific solubility parameters and controlling convective-diffusive flow during deposition, we can direct the assembly of MOF particles into a dense, continuous, and well-adhered selective layer within an asymmetric polymer support, thereby significantly enhancing separation performance and aging resistance.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric polymer supports via phase inversion. A library of solvent pairs (e.g., high-boiling/low-boiling, good/poor polymer solvents) will be used to create MOF suspensions. These will be deposited via a custom flow cell, where parameters like shear rate, residence time, and evaporation rate are precisely controlled. Membrane performance will be evaluated using high-pressure mixed-gas (e.g., CO2/CH4) permeation tests. Characterization will include SEM, XRD, and physical aging studies to correlate solvent-flow conditions with MOF layer morphology, crystallinity, and stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework linking solvent thermodynamics and hydrodynamics to MOF deposition quality in MMMs. It will provide concrete guidelines for selecting solvent pairs and flow parameters to optimize interface formation, leading to membranes with simultaneously enhanced selectivity and permeability that are resistant to physical aging.
proposals.novelty: While solvent choice is known to affect phase inversion, its deliberate pairing and coupling with controlled flow dynamics to *direct* MOF assembly and adhesion in an asymmetric membrane matrix is a novel, physical processing approach distinct from simple blending or post-synthesis modification.

proposals.title: Layer-by-Layer Assembly of Ultrathin MOF Membranes within Asymmetric Polymer Hollow Fibers for Enhanced Gas Separation Performance
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic framework (MOF) membranes offer exceptional molecular sieving potential for energy-efficient gas separations, but their practical implementation is hindered by challenges in forming continuous, defect-free selective layers on complex supports. Pre-2026, conventional in-situ growth or seeding methods often struggle to achieve uniform, sub-micron MOF layers within the porous substructure of high-performance asymmetric hollow fibers, which are the preferred industrial format. A method to precisely control MOF deposition inside the fiber's porous matrix is needed to maximize selectivity without sacrificing permeance.
proposals.hypothesis: Sequential layer-by-layer (LbL) adsorption of MOF precursors or nanocrystals within the mesoporous skin layer of an asymmetric polymer hollow fiber will enable the precise, conformal growth of a continuous, ultrathin MOF separation layer, leading to a composite membrane with superior gas pair selectivity (e.g., CO2/CH4, O2/N2) compared to the pristine polymer or conventionally modified fibers.
proposals.method: We will employ a cyclic, automated dip-coating process on the lumen side of tailor-made asymmetric Matrimid® or polyimide hollow fibers. Each cycle will involve sequential exposure to solutions containing the metal ion (e.g., Zn2+ for ZIF-8) and the organic linker (2-methylimidazole), with intermediate solvent rinses to remove unreacted precursors. The process will be conducted at room temperature to leverage physical adsorption and confined reaction within the fiber's nanopores. We will systematically vary the number of LbL cycles (10-100), solution concentrations, and employ post-synthesis above-Tg thermal annealing (inspired by Koros's recent work) to stabilize the MOF-polymer interface and mitigate physical aging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, scalable route for fabricating MOF-polymer composite hollow fiber membranes with precisely engineered sub-100 nm selective layers. It will provide fundamental insights into the growth mechanics of MOFs in confined polymeric nanopores via LbL processes. The resulting membranes are expected to demonstrate a simultaneous enhancement in gas selectivity and permeance, advancing the state-of-the-art for carbon capture and natural gas purification.
proposals.novelty: While LbL assembly is known for polyelectrolytes, its application for the direct, conformal build-up of a crystalline MOF separation layer *inside* the porous skin of an asymmetric hollow fiber is unexplored. This approach merges the precise nanoscale control of LbL techniques with the high-performance, industrially relevant asymmetric fiber platform central to Koros's research.

proposals.title: In-Situ Growth and Transport Characterization of MOF Membranes in Confined Nanofluidic Channels
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic framework (MOF) membranes show exceptional promise for molecular separations, but their performance is governed by complex, poorly understood nucleation and growth phenomena at the nanoscale. Traditional bulk synthesis and ex-situ characterization fail to isolate the fundamental interfacial reactions and single-pore transport mechanisms that dictate membrane selectivity and permeability. A platform enabling direct, in-situ observation of these processes is critically needed to guide the rational design of next-generation membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that confining MOF growth to well-defined, single nanofluidic channels will enable precise control over nucleation kinetics and crystal orientation, leading to defect-free, single-crystalline MOF structures with transport properties that deviate predictably from bulk models due to extreme confinement and interfacial effects.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric, slit-like nanofluidic channels (5-50 nm height) in silicon or glass using standard lithography and bonding techniques. The channel interior will be functionalized with self-assembled monolayers to serve as a substrate. Precursor solutions will be introduced via pressure-driven flow, and the interfacial reaction for prototypical MOFs (e.g., ZIF-8, HKUST-1) will be initiated and monitored in real-time using high-resolution fluorescence microscopy (for tagged linkers) and interferometric scattering microscopy. Post-growth, single-channel gas (e.g., CO2/CH4) and vapor transport will be measured using a custom, high-pressure permeation system integrated with the nanofluidic chip, correlating growth conditions with separation performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for studying membrane formation at the most fundamental, single-pore level. It will yield the first direct correlations between confined synthesis conditions, nanoscale MOF structure, and intrinsic transport properties, providing quantitative design rules for advanced composite and asymmetric membranes. The insights will directly inform strategies to mitigate physical aging and enhance stability in ultra-thin selective layers.
proposals.novelty: While nanofluidics has been used for biomolecular studies, its application to in-situ, controlled growth and subsequent gas transport measurement of MOFs is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond traditional membrane characterization by isolating and probing the elementary building block—a single crystalline domain in a defined confinement—bridging materials synthesis with fundamental nanofluidics and transport theory.

proposals.title: Covalently-Bonded Molecular Sieve Fillers in Asymmetric Polyimide Membranes for Enhanced Aging Resistance and Gas Separation Performance
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) suffer from interfacial defects and physical aging, which degrade separation performance over time. Pre-2026 work, including Koros's studies on aging and nanoparticle stabilization, highlights the need for robust filler-polymer interfaces. Chemically bonding the filler to the matrix could simultaneously suppress interfacial voids and restrict polymer chain mobility, addressing both key stability challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: Covalent tethering of surface-functionalized molecular sieve nanoparticles (e.g., SAPO-34, ZIF-8) to a high-performance polyimide (e.g., 6FDA-DAM) will create a stabilized, defect-free interface that mitigates physical aging and enhances the selective transport of gas pairs like CO2/CH4 and O2/N2.
proposals.method: We will synthesize amine- or epoxy-functionalized molecular sieve nanoparticles via silane chemistry (e.g., (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane). These will be reacted with a polyimide precursor (polyamic acid) or a functionalized polymer (e.g., anhydride-terminated) to form covalent imide or amide linkages during membrane fabrication. Asymmetric hollow fiber membranes will be spun via a dry-jet/wet-quench process, leveraging Koros's expertise. Performance will be evaluated through pure- and mixed-gas permeation tests before and after accelerated aging (elevated temperature/pressure), with characterization via FTIR, XRD, and TEM.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct materials-based strategy to decouple the trade-off between permeability and stability in MMMs. It will provide fundamental insights into how covalent interfaces influence aging dynamics and gas transport, advancing the design of next-generation membranes for carbon capture and natural gas purification.
proposals.novelty: While surface modification of fillers is known, directly integrating them as covalent cross-link nodes within a high-performance asymmetric membrane architecture is unexplored. This approach uniquely targets the dual root causes of performance decay—interface defects and polymer relaxation—in a single, scalable fabrication step.

proposals.title: Plasticizer Engineering in Asymmetric Polymeric Membranes: A Systematic Study to Decouple and Optimize Adhesion and Gas Separation Performance
proposals.motivation: The fabrication of high-performance asymmetric membranes, a core theme in Koros's work, often requires a delicate balance between achieving defect-free selective layers and maintaining robust adhesion to porous supports. Plasticizers are critical for processing, but their selection and loading are typically empirical, potentially compromising long-term physical stability (aging) or separation performance. A systematic understanding of how plasticizer chemistry and concentration influence both adhesion and intrinsic separation properties is needed to rationally design membranes for demanding applications like natural gas purification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically varying the chemical nature (e.g., polarity, molecular size, volatility) and concentration of plasticizers in glassy polymer dope solutions, we can decouple and independently optimize the adhesion of the resulting asymmetric selective layer to its support and its intrinsic gas separation performance, moving beyond a simple trade-off relationship.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric hollow fiber membranes using a polymer of intrinsic microporosity (PIM-1) or a high-performance polyimide as the base material. A series of plasticizers (e.g., dioctyl phthalate, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone, polyethylene glycol) will be incorporated at varying loadings (5-30 wt%) into the dope solution. Membrane morphology will be characterized via SEM. Adhesion strength will be quantitatively measured using a custom peel/shear test apparatus. Gas permeation (CO2, CH4, N2, O2) and aging behavior will be tracked over 100+ hours. Data will be analyzed using response surface methodology to map the multi-variable design space and identify optimal formulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework linking plasticizer selection to two critical but often conflicting membrane properties: adhesion and separation performance. It will yield design rules for formulating dope solutions that minimize delamination risks in modules while maintaining or enhancing selectivity and permeability, directly addressing stability challenges in industrial membrane systems.
proposals.novelty: While plasticizers are known to affect morphology, a targeted study that quantitatively correlates specific plasticizer parameters to both mechanical adhesion metrics and gas transport properties in asymmetric membranes is novel. This moves beyond viewing plasticizers solely as processing aids to treating them as critical design variables for holistic membrane performance.

proposals.title: Scalable Fabrication of High-Performance Asymmetric Composite Membranes via Controlled Solvent Evaporation and Physical Aging
proposals.motivation: The transition from lab-scale to industrially relevant fabrication of high-performance composite membranes is a critical bottleneck. Current film casting methods for creating thin, selective layers on porous supports often fail to maintain the precise microstructure and performance when scaled, limiting the commercial deployment of advanced gas and solvent separation membranes. A robust, scalable process is needed to produce defect-free, asymmetric composite membranes with consistent separation properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely controlling the solvent evaporation kinetics and subsequent physical aging environment during the scale-up of a modified film casting process, we can reproducibly fabricate composite membranes that retain the superior separation performance and stability observed at the lab scale.
proposals.method: We will adapt a lab-scale doctor blade casting system for continuous, wider-web coating onto commercial polymeric supports. The process will integrate in-line monitoring of coating thickness and solvent concentration. Key variables include casting solution composition (inspired by Koros's work with high-performance polymers and natural aging), controlled evaporation chamber atmosphere (temperature, humidity, and gas sweep), and a post-casting above-Tg annealing step to manage physical aging. Performance will be evaluated using high-pressure mixed-gas permeation tests for gas separations (e.g., CO2/CH4) and pervaporation tests for solvent purification.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, scalable fabrication protocol for next-generation composite membranes, directly addressing a major translational challenge. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay between processing conditions, thin-film microstructure evolution, and final separation performance, bridging materials science and chemical engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated approach to scale-up, which explicitly couples controlled evaporation with tailored physical aging protocols—a strategy informed by recent pre-2026 findings on aging benefits—to preserve the delicate microstructure of advanced separation layers during manufacturing.

proposals.title: Targeted Gas Separations via Plasticized High-Tg Polymer Matrices with Engineered Zeolite Molecular Sieves
proposals.motivation: High-performance glassy polymers are essential for energy-efficient membrane-based gas separations, but they often face a fundamental trade-off between permeability and selectivity. The incorporation of molecular sieving fillers, such as zeolites, into plasticized high-Tg polymer matrices offers a promising route to decouple this trade-off by creating hybrid materials with tailored transport pathways. This approach can address critical separations like CO2/CH4, O2/N2, and olefin/paraffin, which are vital for natural gas upgrading, air separation, and chemical processing.
proposals.hypothesis: The strategic selection of zeolite fillers (e.g., SAPO-34 for CO2/CH4, DDR for CO2/N2, or AlPO-18 for olefin/paraffin) within a plasticized, high-Tg polymer matrix (e.g., polyimide or PIM) will create synergistic, size- and affinity-selective pathways that significantly enhance both permeability and selectivity for targeted gas pairs compared to the neat polymer or conventional mixed-matrix membranes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize asymmetric mixed-matrix membranes using high-Tg polymers (e.g., 6FDA-DAM polyimide or a polymer of intrinsic microporosity) plasticized with thermally stable, high-boiling-point additives identified from prior work. Specific, commercially available small-pore zeolites (SAPO-34, DDR, AlPO-18) will be incorporated at varying loadings (5-30 wt%). Membranes will be fabricated via a dry-jet wet phase inversion process to form an integral asymmetric structure. Performance will be evaluated using pure and mixed-gas permeation tests for key separations (CO2/CH4, O2/N2, C3H6/C3H8). Physical aging behavior and filler-polymer interface quality will be characterized using DSC, positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS), and SEM.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework for rationally designing hybrid membranes by pairing specific molecular sieve functionalities with a plasticized, processable high-Tg polymer platform. It will provide fundamental insights into how filler geometry and surface chemistry influence gas transport and physical aging in these complex matrices. The results will advance the field of mixed-matrix membranes by demonstrating a targeted, separation-specific enhancement strategy beyond generic permeability/selectivity improvements.
proposals.novelty: While mixed-matrix membranes are well-studied, the combination of a *plasticized* high-Tg polymer matrix with *specifically selected* small-pore zeolites to create targeted, synergistic separation pathways is novel. This approach moves beyond using fillers as generic permeability enhancers, instead leveraging their precise molecular sieving properties within a matrix whose chain mobility and free volume are uniquely tuned by plasticization.

proposals.title: In-Situ Sol-Gel Synthesis of Molecular Sieve Domains within Asymmetric Polymer Membranes for Enhanced Gas Separation Performance and Aging Resistance
proposals.motivation: Hybrid organic-inorganic membranes offer a promising route to surpass the Robeson upper bound for gas separations, but conventional blending of pre-synthesized fillers often leads to poor interfacial adhesion, particle agglomeration, and performance decay. Pre-2026 work, including Koros's studies on aging and asymmetric structures, highlights the need for robust, well-integrated composite materials. An in-situ synthesis approach could create a more intimate and stable hybrid structure, directly addressing interfacial defects and physical aging challenges in high-performance membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the in-situ sol-gel growth of molecular sieve phases (e.g., silica or organosilica networks) within the porous substructure of an asymmetric polymer membrane will create a seamlessly integrated hybrid selective layer, leading to simultaneous enhancement in gas-pair selectivity, permeability, and long-term stability against physical aging.
proposals.method: We will fabricate an asymmetric polyimide or polymer of intrinsic microporosity (PIM) membrane via phase inversion to form a porous support. The sol-gel precursor solution (e.g., tetraethoxysilane or vinyltrimethoxysilane with controlled hydrolysis) will be infiltrated into the polymer matrix. Subsequent controlled condensation and drying, potentially followed by above-Tg thermal annealing as inspired by recent Koros work, will grow a molecularly engineered inorganic network within the polymer's free volume and pores. Characterization will involve SEM/EDS, FTIR, positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) for pore structure, and detailed gas permeation tests (CO2/CH4, O2/N2) before and after accelerated aging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel fabrication paradigm for hybrid membranes, moving beyond simple blending to in-situ creation of the sieve phase. It is expected to yield a fundamental understanding of the relationship between in-situ synthesis conditions, hybrid morphology, and transport-stability properties. The project will deliver a membrane platform with demonstrably improved performance and aging resistance for energy-intensive gas separations.
proposals.novelty: While sol-gel coatings and mixed-matrix membranes are known, the non-obvious aspect is the targeted, confined growth of the molecular sieve phase specifically within the pre-formed asymmetric polymer matrix, leveraging its morphology to template and anchor the inorganic network, thereby unifying the concepts of asymmetric structure, hybrid materials, and aging mitigation.

proposals.title: Adhesion-Engineered Mixed-Matrix Membranes as Selective Interfaces for High-Fidelity Chemiresistive Sensors
proposals.motivation: Chemiresistive sensors for volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and toxic gases often lack the necessary selectivity for complex mixtures, limiting their use in industrial safety and environmental monitoring. Mixed-matrix materials (MMMs) have revolutionized membrane-based gas separations by combining the processability of polymers with the precise sieving properties of molecular sieves or nanoparticles. Translating this adhesion-controlled MMM concept to sensor interfaces offers a promising route to impart molecular-level selectivity directly onto a transduction platform.
proposals.hypothesis: By precisely controlling the polymer-filler interfacial adhesion in a mixed-matrix composite, we can engineer a selective surface layer that preferentially concentrates target analytes at a chemiresistive element, thereby dramatically enhancing sensor selectivity without sacrificing sensitivity or response time.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric MMM films with an ultrathin, adhesion-controlled selective skin layer atop a porous support, adapting techniques from Koros's work on CMS membranes. The selective layer will integrate SBA-15-based amine sorbents (for CO2) or tailored nanoparticles within a glassy polymer matrix (e.g., Matrimid®). Adhesion will be controlled via silane coupling agents, inspired by the VTMS treatment in recent sorbent work. These films will be integrated as the active element in a micro-fabricated chemiresistive sensor platform, where analyte permeation and surface adsorption modulate electrical resistance. Performance will be evaluated against gas mixtures (e.g., CO2/N2, benzene/cyclohexane) to quantify selectivity enhancement.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for chemical sensors by directly applying advanced membrane material science. It will yield a framework for rationally designing sensor interfaces with membrane-like selectivity and demonstrate a tangible, high-performance sensor prototype for a challenging separation, such as discriminating between aromatic and aliphatic VOCs.
proposals.novelty: While MMMs are well-established for separations, their application as engineered, adhesion-controlled selective interfaces for chemiresistive sensors is unexplored. This approach moves beyond simple bulk composite sensing materials to create a functionally graded, asymmetric structure that mimics a membrane's selective skin.

proposals.title: Continuous Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) Matrices with Dispersed Polymer Particles as a Novel Platform for High-Flux, Aging-Resistant Gas Separation Membranes
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) face intrinsic trade-offs between permeability and selectivity, often limited by poor polymer-filler interfacial compatibility and aging-induced performance decay. Inverting the conventional architecture to create a continuous, highly selective MOF phase could leverage the superior, well-defined porosity of MOFs for transport while using dispersed polymer particles to seal grain boundaries and enhance mechanical stability, addressing key challenges in membrane durability and performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A continuous, percolating MOF matrix with finely dispersed, cross-linked polymer particles as a 'filler' will yield a composite membrane with gas transport properties dominated by the MOF's intrinsic selectivity, while the polymer phase mitigates aging by stabilizing the microstructure against physical aging and interfacial defects.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the composite via a stepwise, in-situ method: first, preparing a colloidal suspension of pre-formed, cross-linked polymer nanoparticles (e.g., VTMS-treated polyimide for enhanced CO2 affinity). Second, this suspension will be used as the dispersion medium for the controlled, solvothermal growth of a prototypical high-performance MOF (e.g., ZIF-8 or HKUST-1), forming a continuous MOF matrix around the polymer particles. Asymmetric hollow fiber supports will be fabricated, and the composite layer will be applied as a thin selective skin. Characterization will involve positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) for free volume, in-situ X-ray diffraction for structural stability, and detailed gas permeation tests (CO2/CH4, O2/N2) under varied pressure and humidity to assess separation performance and physical aging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for composite membrane design, moving beyond polymer-centric MMMs. It is expected to demonstrate a pathway to membranes with performance approaching the upper bounds defined by the pure MOF, but with significantly improved mechanical integrity and resistance to physical aging, directly addressing durability concerns for industrial gas separation.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the complete inversion of the traditional MMM structure: the MOF is not a dispersed filler but the continuous separation medium, while the polymer acts as a stabilizing, defect-healing dispersed phase. This fundamentally alters transport pathways and aging mechanisms compared to all prior work.

proposals.title: Decoupling Particle Size and Interface Effects: A Systematic Study of MOF Morphology on Aging-Resistant Mixed-Matrix Membrane Performance
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) incorporating MOF fillers offer a promising route to surpass the polymer performance upper bound for gas separations like CO2/CH4. However, empirical filler selection often conflates particle size, morphology, and surface chemistry, leading to suboptimal performance and poor understanding of physical aging, a critical issue for glassy polymers. A systematic, decoupled study is needed to isolate the role of particle size and shape on transport and long-term stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given MOF chemistry, an optimal particle size and morphology exists that maximizes separation performance by balancing selective pathway density with interfacial defect minimization, and that this optimal filler geometry will also impart significant resistance to physical aging in the polymer matrix.
proposals.method: We will synthesize or source a series of ZIF-8 particles with controlled sizes (e.g., 50 nm, 200 nm, 500 nm) and morphologies (cubic, rhombic dodecahedron) while maintaining identical surface chemistry via post-synthetic modification. These will be incorporated into a high-performance, asymmetric polyimide membrane matrix (e.g., 6FDA-DAM) at fixed loadings. Membrane performance will be evaluated using high-pressure mixed-gas permeation tests (CO2/CH4). We will employ accelerated aging protocols (elevated temperature) and characterize the physical aging kinetics via gas permeability decay and positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) to probe interfacial free volume.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework linking MOF particle geometry to separation performance and aging resistance, moving beyond trial-and-error filler selection. It will yield design rules for creating MMMs with simultaneously high and stable performance, directly addressing a key industrial challenge for carbon capture and natural gas purification.
proposals.novelty: While MOF-polymer compatibility is well-studied, systematically decoupling particle size from shape and surface chemistry to isolate their individual effects on transport and physical aging is a novel and necessary step for rational MMM design. The explicit focus on aging, a core theme of Koros's recent work, applied to morphology-controlled fillers is unexplored.

proposals.title: Rigid Metal-Organic Polyhedra as Molecular Scaffolds to Arrest Physical Aging in Mixed Matrix Membranes for Gas Separations
proposals.motivation: Physical aging in high-performance glassy polymer membranes, such as polyimides and polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs), leads to a time-dependent densification and loss of free volume, degrading gas permeability and selectivity. This aging is a critical barrier to the long-term industrial deployment of these materials. Mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) incorporating fillers like MOFs or zeolites can improve performance but often do not fundamentally inhibit the underlying polymer chain relaxation responsible for aging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding rigid, nanoscale Metal-Organic Polyhedra (MOPs) as a dispersed, interconnected scaffold will act as a 'molecular rebar,' physically constraining polymer chain segmental mobility and thereby dramatically suppressing physical aging while maintaining or enhancing separation performance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize rigid MOPs (e.g., Cu24(m-BDC)24 or Rh-based MOPs known for high stability). These will be incorporated at low loadings (1-10 wt%) into aging-prone polymer matrices (e.g., 6FDA-DAM polyimide or a PIM). Membranes will be fabricated via solution casting. Aging will be monitored via systematic gas permeation (O2, N2, CO2, CH4) over 6-12 months. Complementary characterization will include positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) for free volume element analysis, dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) for segmental mobility, and wide-angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) to assess chain packing.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for creating aging-resistant MMMs by using rigid, molecular-scale scaffolds to impede polymer relaxation. It will provide fundamental insights into the relationship between filler topology, polymer-filler interface, and long-term transport stability. The successful outcome would deliver a membrane platform with sustained separation performance, a key advancement for practical gas separation processes.
proposals.novelty: While MOPs have been explored for their porosity, their use as rigid, non-porous scaffolds specifically to arrest physical aging in glassy polymers is novel. This 'molecular rebar' concept directly targets the mechanistic root of aging (chain mobility) rather than merely adding porosity, representing a distinct strategy from prior MMM work focused on molecular sieving or sorption enhancement.

proposals.title: Microfluidic Templating of Asymmetric Polymer Membranes with Ordered, High-Loading MOF Domains for Enhanced Gas Separation Performance
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) combining polymers with metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offer a promising route to surpass the Robeson upper bound for gas separations like CO2/CH4. However, conventional fabrication methods often lead to MOF aggregation, poor polymer-filler interface quality, and non-uniform filler distribution, which compromise performance and exacerbate physical aging. Pre-2026, achieving high, ordered MOF loadings in a controlled, defect-free morphology remains a critical challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using microfluidic droplet templating to create a precursor emulsion with MOF particles confined within discrete, ordered aqueous phases will enable the fabrication of asymmetric polymer membranes with continuous, high-loading, and spatially organized MOF-rich domains, leading to superior and more stable gas separation performance.
proposals.method: We will employ a water-in-oil microfluidic device to generate monodisperse aqueous droplets containing suspended MOF nanoparticles (e.g., ZIF-8). This emulsion will be cast onto a support, where the continuous oil phase—comprising a polymer (e.g., 6FDA-DAM) and a volatile solvent—will undergo phase inversion to form the asymmetric membrane structure. The aqueous droplets will template the formation of ordered, MOF-filled domains within the polymer matrix. Key parameters like flow rate ratios, MOF concentration, and polymer dope composition will be systematically varied. The resulting membranes will be characterized using SEM, XRD, and gas permeation tests (CO2, CH4, N2) before and after accelerated aging protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel processing platform for creating MMMs with unprecedented control over filler morphology and distribution. It is expected to yield membranes with enhanced selectivity and permeability due to optimized percolation pathways and reduced interfacial defects, while also demonstrating improved resistance to physical aging. The findings will provide fundamental insights into the relationship between templated microstructure and long-term separation performance.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics has been used for particle synthesis and emulsion generation, its direct application to template the internal, ordered morphology of a high-performance asymmetric gas separation membrane is novel. This approach moves beyond simple blending to actively engineer the spatial arrangement of MOF domains within a polymer matrix, a significant departure from pre-2026 MMM fabrication norms.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation of Zeolitic and MOF Fillers vs. Activated Carbon in Asymmetric Polyethersulfone Membranes for Enhanced Gas Separation Performance and Aging Resistance
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) incorporating activated carbon (AC) fillers in glassy polymers like polyethersulfone (PES) are promising for energy-efficient gas separations. However, AC's broad pore size distribution and surface chemistry may not be optimal for achieving the ultimate combination of high selectivity, permeability, and long-term physical aging resistance. A direct, systematic comparison with engineered microporous fillers like zeolites and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) is needed to identify the most effective filler class.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineered microporous fillers (e.g., SAPO-34 zeolite and ZIF-8 MOF) will outperform conventional activated carbon in PES-based MMMs by providing superior molecular sieving, stronger polymer-filler interfacial interactions, and greater suppression of physical aging, leading to more stable and selective gas transport.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of asymmetric, flat-sheet MMMs via phase inversion, incorporating 10-30 wt% of three fillers: a commercial activated carbon, SAPO-34 zeolite, and ZIF-8 MOF, into a PES matrix. Membrane morphology will be characterized by SEM. Pure-gas (CO2, CH4, N2, O2) permeation tests will be conducted at 35°C and up to 10 bar to evaluate initial permeability and selectivity. Accelerated physical aging studies will be performed by monitoring gas permeation properties over time for membranes stored under vacuum at 35°C, a temperature above the polymer's Tg to assess the stabilizing effect of different fillers as suggested by Koros's recent work on above-Tg annealing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, head-to-head performance ranking of three major porous filler classes in a relevant polymer platform, directly linking filler properties to separation performance and physical aging. It will establish clear selection criteria for filler choice in next-generation MMMs for gas separations like CO2/CH4 and O2/N2.
proposals.novelty: While individual filler types have been studied, a controlled comparative study challenging the ubiquitous activated carbon with precisely engineered zeolites and MOFs in the same polymer and morphology is novel. The focus on aging resistance in asymmetric membranes, informed by recent insights into above-Tg annealing, moves beyond typical initial performance metrics.

proposals.title: Decoupling Physical Aging and Conditioning in Asymmetric Carbon Molecular Sieve Membranes Under Continuous Mixed-Gas Feed
proposals.motivation: The long-term performance stability of high-performance asymmetric carbon molecular sieve (CMS) membranes is a critical barrier to industrial adoption. While physical aging of glassy polymers is well-studied, the evolution of permeability and selectivity in CMS membranes under realistic, continuous mixed-gas feeds and transmembrane pressures remains poorly understood, particularly for asymmetric structures. This gap prevents accurate lifetime prediction and process design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the long-term gas separation performance of asymmetric CMS membranes under continuous operation is governed by a competition between physical aging, which reduces permeability, and conditioning effects from strongly adsorbing gases like CO2, which can initially enhance it. The net effect will depend on membrane structure, feed composition, and pressure.
proposals.method: We will fabricate flat-sheet asymmetric CMS membranes via pyrolysis of a nanoparticle-stabilized polyimide precursor, leveraging Koros's recent above-Tg annealing insights. Membranes will be subjected to long-term (1000+ hour) tests with continuous feeds of CO2/CH4 and O2/N2 mixtures at industrially relevant pressures (10-30 bar). Permeability and selectivity will be monitored in situ via gas chromatography. Post-mortem characterization will include X-ray scattering and porosimetry to correlate performance drift with structural changes in the selective skin and porous support.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed kinetic model for performance evolution in asymmetric CMS membranes under realistic conditions, separating aging from conditioning effects. It will yield design rules for stabilizing membrane performance through tailored pyrolysis protocols and module operation strategies, directly addressing a key commercialization hurdle.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in focusing on asymmetric CMS membranes—where the support may constrain aging in the skin layer—and in using continuous mixed-gas feeds at high pressure to probe non-equilibrium aging phenomena, moving beyond typical pure-gas, low-pressure studies on dense films.

proposals.title: Decoupling Adsorption and Diffusion Contributions to Mixed-Gas Permeation in High-Performance Polymer and Carbon Molecular Sieve Membranes
proposals.motivation: The performance of advanced gas separation membranes, including polymer and carbon molecular sieve (CMS) types, is typically described by the solution-diffusion model, where selectivity is assumed to be dominated by differences in diffusivity. However, under industrially relevant high-pressure and mixed-gas conditions, competitive adsorption effects may become significant, potentially challenging this diffusion-centric paradigm and leading to performance predictions that deviate from pure-gas experiments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for glassy polymer and CMS membranes designed for kinetically controlled separations (e.g., CO2/CH4, O2/N2), competitive adsorption under high-pressure, mixed-gas conditions introduces a non-negligible, and sometimes dominant, thermodynamic contribution to overall selectivity that is not captured by pure-gas diffusion measurements.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of asymmetric hollow fiber membranes from a polyimide precursor (e.g., 6FDA-DAM) and subject them to controlled pyrolysis to create CMS membranes with tuned ultramicropores. High-pressure (up to 30 bar) pure- and mixed-gas permeation tests for CO2/CH4 will be conducted. The experimental permeation data will be rigorously analyzed using the Dual-Mode Sorption/Partial Immobilization model. Key parameters (sorption isotherms, diffusion coefficients, and permeability) will be decoupled for each gas component under mixed-gas conditions to quantify the relative contributions of adsorption (solubility selectivity) and diffusion (mobility selectivity) to the observed overall selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fundamental, quantitative framework for separating adsorption and diffusion effects in complex membrane separations. It will deliver critical guidance for membrane material design and process modeling by identifying conditions where adsorption cannot be ignored, thereby improving the accuracy of translating laboratory single-gas results to real-world mixed-gas performance.
proposals.novelty: While adsorption is acknowledged in rubbery polymers, its systematic quantification under non-ideal, high-pressure conditions in rigid, diffusion-selective glassy and CMS membranes—materials explicitly engineered to maximize diffusivity differences—represents a significant and underexplored challenge to conventional performance analysis.

proposals.title: Solvent-Engineered Synthesis of Asymmetric Carbon Molecular Sieve Membranes for Enhanced Gas Permeance
proposals.motivation: Aqueous-phase synthesis of polymeric precursors for carbon molecular sieve (CMS) membranes is common but can limit performance. Residual water can create macrovoids or disrupt polymer chain packing during phase inversion, leading to substructural defects that reduce selectivity or necessitate thicker selective layers to compensate, lowering permeance. Exploring alternative synthesis routes addresses a fundamental materials processing limitation to push the performance boundaries of next-generation membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: Non-aqueous or solvent-free synthesis of polyimide precursors will yield more homogeneous, defect-free asymmetric structures with finer pore architecture after pyrolysis, leading to CMS membranes with significantly higher gas permeance while maintaining or improving selectivity compared to aquatically derived analogues.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of asymmetric membranes from a standard polyimide (e.g., 6FDA-DAM) using (1) a traditional water-based coagulation bath, (2) a solvent-exchange process with non-aqueous coagulants (e.g., methanol, isopropanol), and (3) a physical solvent-vapor-induced phase separation (SVIPS) technique. All precursors will undergo identical, controlled pyrolysis protocols (e.g., 550-800°C under inert gas) to form CMS membranes. Critical aging phenomena and performance will be characterized using high-pressure pure- and mixed-gas permeation tests (CO2, CH4, N2, O2), positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) for ultramicropore analysis, and electron microscopy for morphology evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal link between precursor synthesis solvent environment and final CMS membrane performance, providing a new processing lever for performance optimization. It will deliver a framework for 'solvent engineering' the pyrolysis precursor to achieve higher permeance, directly impacting energy efficiency for large-scale gas separations like CO2 capture and natural gas purification.
proposals.novelty: While non-aqueous coagulation is known for some polymers, its systematic application and mechanistic study for CMS membrane precursors, combined with the assessment of physical aging stability in the resulting carbon structures, is unexplored. This approach integrates Koros's expertise in asymmetric structures, aging, and high-performance membranes with a novel processing variable.

proposals.title: High-Flux ZIF-8 Membranes on Asymmetric Hollow Fiber Supports: Synthesis, Aging, and Module-Relevant Gas Separation Performance
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic framework (MOF) membranes, particularly ZIF-8, show exceptional promise for energy-efficient gas separations like propylene/propane. However, most research uses flat-sheet supports, which are not optimal for industrial-scale modules. Developing high-performance, defect-free ZIF-8 membranes on high-surface-area, mechanically robust hollow fiber supports is a critical step toward practical implementation, directly addressing scalability challenges in membrane-based separation processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging the high-surface-area, asymmetric morphology of polymeric hollow fiber supports and applying controlled, above-Tg thermal annealing protocols, we can synthesize thin, selective, and aging-resistant ZIF-8 membranes that demonstrate stable, module-relevant separation performance under mixed-gas conditions.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric polyimide or polyethersulfone hollow fibers via phase inversion. ZIF-8 membranes will be grown via secondary seeded growth or contra-diffusion methods, optimizing precursor concentration and cycling. A key focus will be applying controlled thermal annealing (above the polymer's Tg but below its degradation temperature) to the composite membrane to mitigate physical aging of the support and enhance ZIF-8 membrane stability. Performance will be evaluated using single and mixed-gas permeation tests (e.g., H2/CH4, CO2/CH4, C3H6/C3H8) before and after aging studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable pathway for manufacturing high-performance MOF membranes by demonstrating a successful transition from model flat supports to industrially relevant hollow fiber geometries. It will yield fundamental insights into the interplay between polymeric support aging, MOF membrane formation, and long-term separation performance, directly informing module design principles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the intentional integration of Koros-group expertise in asymmetric hollow fiber supports, physical aging mitigation via annealing, and MOF membrane synthesis to solve a scale-up bottleneck, moving beyond conventional flat-sheet demonstrations to create module-ready configurations.

proposals.title: A Robust, Real-Time Propylene Concentration Sensor Based on Selective Permeation Through an Asymmetric Carbon Molecular Sieve Membrane
proposals.motivation: Real-time monitoring of propylene (C3H6) concentration is critical for process control and safety in petrochemical plants and polymer production. Existing sensors often lack selectivity in complex hydrocarbon streams or suffer from drift. A membrane-based sensor exploiting intrinsic transport selectivity offers a potentially more stable and specific analytical modality, aligning with the need for advanced process analytical technology (PAT).
proposals.hypothesis: The steady-state permeation flux of a gas mixture through a highly C3H6-selective membrane can be correlated directly and robustly to the C3H6 partial pressure in the feed, enabling its use as a concentration sensor even against a background of other light hydrocarbons.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric carbon molecular sieve (CMS) membranes via pyrolysis of a 6FDA-based polyimide precursor, employing above-Tg annealing protocols to stabilize microstructure and enhance C3H6/C3H8 selectivity. The sensor will consist of a precisely characterized CMS membrane disc separating a high-pressure feed chamber from a swept, low-pressure permeate side. Real-time C3H6 concentration will be inferred by measuring downstream permeate composition via mass spectrometry and correlating it with a pre-calibrated permeation model that accounts for competitive sorption and partial pressure driving force.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for using highly selective membranes as continuous composition sensors, moving beyond their traditional separation function. It will deliver a prototype sensor demonstrating the principle for C3H6 and provide new insights into the stability and aging behavior of CMS films under dynamic, mixed-gas sensing conditions.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the conventional use of membranes from separators to analytical sensing elements, specifically targeting the challenging olefin/paraffin pair. The approach leverages Koros's recent advances in stabilizing CMS membranes via annealing to address the critical sensor drift problem inherent in polymeric materials.

proposals.title: Aqueous-Synthesized ZIF-8 Membranes for the Selective Separation of Organic Liquid Isomers via Molecular Sieving
proposals.motivation: The separation of organic liquid isomers, such as xylene isomers or linear/branched alkanes, is critical for petrochemical and pharmaceutical industries but remains energy-intensive due to their similar physical properties. Membranes offer a low-energy alternative, but achieving precise molecular sieving in the liquid phase is challenging. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like ZIF-8, with its ~3.4 Å pore aperture, show promise for gas separations, but its potential for liquid-phase organic separations, especially via scalable aqueous synthesis routes, is largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that continuous, defect-free ZIF-8 membranes synthesized via an aqueous, scalable route will exhibit selective molecular sieving for organic liquid isomers based on subtle differences in molecular size and shape, with performance governed by framework rigidity and stability under liquid exposure.
proposals.method: We will synthesize ZIF-8 membranes on asymmetric polymeric supports using the reported aqueous synthesis method at room temperature. Membrane quality will be assessed via SEM, XRD, and single-gas permeation tests. Liquid separation performance will be evaluated for model mixtures (e.g., p-xylene/o-xylene or n-hexane/2,2-dimethylbutane) using a custom pervaporation setup. We will systematically study the effects of 'above-Tg annealing' of the polymeric support (a Koros-group method) on membrane adhesion and stability, and employ physical aging protocols to stabilize the composite structure under aggressive organic solvents.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental understanding of molecular transport and sieving mechanisms of organic liquids in ZIF-8, moving beyond its established gas separation role. It will demonstrate the viability of scalable, aqueous-synthesized MOF membranes for challenging liquid separations, providing a performance-stability roadmap for hybrid organic-MOF membrane systems.
proposals.novelty: While ZIF-8 gas separation is well-studied, applying the aqueous synthesis route to create membranes for liquid-phase organic isomer separations is novel. Combining this with asymmetric support modification via above-Tg annealing and physical aging to enhance performance in liquids integrates Koros-group expertise in a new, high-impact context.

proposals.title: Continuous Flow Synthesis of High-Performance ZIF-8 Membranes via Roll-to-Roll Interfacial Microfluidic Processing
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic framework (MOF) membranes, particularly ZIF-8, show exceptional promise for energy-efficient gas separations like propylene/propane. However, their commercial viability is hindered by the lack of scalable, reproducible manufacturing techniques. Current batch synthesis methods are incompatible with the high-throughput, low-cost production required for industrial membrane modules.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous flow, roll-to-roll coating process utilizing interfacial microfluidic synthesis can produce high-quality, defect-minimized ZIF-8 membranes with consistent separation performance, by precisely controlling the reaction kinetics and crystallization environment at the polymer support interface.
proposals.method: We will design a roll-to-roll apparatus where a porous asymmetric polymer hollow fiber or flat sheet support is continuously fed through a microfluidic coating module. This module will create a laminar flow interface between separate, metered streams of the zinc precursor and 2-methylimidazole linker solutions, directly initiating ZIF-8 synthesis at the support surface. Key parameters—solution concentration, flow rate, residence time, and support pre-treatment (e.g., silane priming)—will be systematically varied. Membrane performance will be evaluated using high-pressure mixed-gas permeation tests for C3H6/C3H8 separation, with physical characterization (XRD, SEM) linking synthesis conditions to microstructure and aging stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational continuous manufacturing platform for MOF membranes, directly translating lab-scale performance to a scalable process. It will provide a quantitative framework linking flow synthesis parameters to membrane microstructure and separation properties, a critical step towards industrial adoption.
proposals.novelty: While continuous ZIF-8 powder synthesis exists, applying roll-to-roll interfacial microfluidics to directly synthesize and deposit a continuous, selective membrane layer on a flexible support in one step is a novel, non-obvious manufacturing paradigm distinct from conventional secondary growth or dip-coating methods.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation of Poly(ethylene 2,5-furandicarboxylate) (PEF) Membranes for the Selective Barrier Properties Against CO2, Water Vapor, and Organic Vapors
proposals.motivation: Poly(ethylene 2,5-furandicarboxylate) (PEF) is a promising bio-based polymer with exceptional barrier properties against O2 and CO2 compared to PET, as established in packaging literature pre-2026. However, its potential as a selective barrier membrane for gas and vapor separations relevant to chemical processing—such as CO2 capture, dehydration, and organic solvent recovery—remains largely unexplored. A comprehensive study of its transport properties for a wider range of penetrants is needed to assess its viability in advanced membrane applications.
proposals.hypothesis: The rigid, planar furan ring in PEF provides a high-energy barrier for diffusion, leading to superior size-sieving selectivity for small gas pairs (e.g., CO2/CH4) and high resistance to the permeation of condensable vapors (water, organics) due to low sorption, but its performance will be highly sensitive to physical aging and conditioning history.
proposals.method: We will fabricate dense, asymmetric PEF films via solution casting and phase inversion, leveraging Koros's expertise in asymmetric membrane formation. Films will be subjected to controlled above-Tg thermal annealing protocols to stabilize their structure and mitigate physical aging effects. Time-lag and sorption gravimetric techniques will be used to measure pure-gas permeability, diffusivity, and solubility for CO2, CH4, N2, and water vapor. Organic vapor (e.g., n-hexane, ethanol) transport will be studied using a vapor-pressure gradient apparatus to assess selectivity in potential solvent purification scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive database of PEF's barrier properties for industrially relevant gases and vapors beyond O2, directly informing its potential in separation processes. It will elucidate the structure-property relationships linking furan-based rigidity to transport behavior and provide critical insights into the long-term stability of PEF membranes under varying conditioning environments.
proposals.novelty: While PEF's barrier properties for packaging are known, this study systematically re-contextualizes PEF as a potential separation membrane, investigating its performance for gas pairs and vapors critical to industrial separations—a significant departure from existing literature. The explicit focus on aging and annealing effects on transport properties for these penetrants is also novel.

proposals.title: High-Pressure Performance and Aging of Asymmetric Mixed-Matrix Membranes for Natural Gas Upgrading
proposals.motivation: Industrial membrane processes for CO2/CH4 separation, such as natural gas sweetening, often operate at feed pressures exceeding 40 bar to match pipeline and wellhead conditions. However, fundamental academic studies on advanced mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) are typically limited to low-pressure differentials (<4 bar), creating a critical gap between lab-scale performance metrics and real-world operating conditions. This disconnect obscures understanding of membrane plasticization, physical aging, and nanoparticle-polymer interface stability under industrially relevant stresses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying high transmembrane pressure differences (ΔP > 20 bar) will induce distinct, non-linear performance decay and aging mechanisms in asymmetric MMMs compared to low-pressure testing, driven by intensified penetrant-induced swelling and interfacial stress at the polymer-filler interface.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric hollow fiber MMMs using a polymer of intrinsic microporosity (PIM-1) as the continuous phase, incorporating surface-modified SBA-15 or carbon molecular sieve nanoparticles to enhance selectivity. A custom high-pressure permeation system will be built to expose membranes to sustained CO2/CH4 mixed-gas feeds at ΔP from 5 to 30 bar. Performance (permeance, selectivity) will be tracked over extended periods (1000+ hours) using on-line gas chromatography. Post-mortem analysis via positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) and nanoindentation will quantify aging-related changes in free volume and physical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first detailed performance-aging map for high-free-volume MMMs under industrially relevant pressure stresses. It will provide critical data on the upper pressure limits for nanoparticle-stabilized membranes and identify failure modes specific to high-pressure operation. The findings will guide the design of next-generation membranes for high-pressure gas separations by linking processing conditions, filler morphology, and long-term stability.
proposals.novelty: While high-pressure testing is standard for dense films, its systematic application to study aging in complex, asymmetric MMM architectures is novel. The approach uniquely probes the synergy between physical aging, penetrant-induced plasticization, and nanofiller effects under conditions that mimic true process environments, moving beyond idealized low-pressure characterization.

proposals.title: Scalable Synthesis of ZIF-94 via THF/Methanol Solvent Systems for High-Performance Asymmetric Mixed-Matrix Membranes
proposals.motivation: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like ZIF-94 offer exceptional molecular sieving potential for gas separations, but their synthesis often relies on hazardous solvents like dimethylformamide (DMF), limiting safe, economical scale-up for industrial membrane production. A recently reported non-hazardous synthesis using THF/methanol mixtures presents a critical opportunity to bridge lab-scale discovery and practical application. Developing a robust, scalable protocol for this synthesis is essential to produce the quantities of high-quality ZIF-94 needed for fabricating advanced mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) for carbon capture and hydrogen purification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the THF/methanol synthesis of ZIF-94 can be systematically scaled while maintaining precise control over particle size, morphology, and crystallinity, and that these scaled particles will effectively integrate into asymmetric polymer matrices to yield MMMs with superior and stable separation performance under aggressive feed conditions.
proposals.method: We will first optimize the nucleation and growth kinetics of ZIF-94 in THF/methanol via systematic variation of reagent concentration, temperature, and stirring rates in liter-scale batches, using XRD, SEM, and N2 physisorption for characterization. The optimized ZIF-94 will then be incorporated into high-performance polymers (e.g., 6FDA-based polyimides) to form dense film MMMs. These will be cast into integrally skinned asymmetric hollow fiber membranes via a dry-jet wet-quench spinning process, leveraging Koros's expertise in physical aging and asymmetric membrane formation. Membrane performance will be evaluated for CO2/CH4 and H2/CO2 separations before and after controlled physical aging and exposure to simulated humid feeds.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, scalable synthesis route for a key MOF using green(er) solvents, directly addressing a major materials bottleneck in membrane manufacturing. It will demonstrate the translation of this material into practical asymmetric hollow fiber MMMs, providing critical data on the interplay between scalable particle synthesis, membrane processing, and long-term separation stability under realistic conditions.
proposals.novelty: While the THF/methanol synthesis is known, its systematic scale-up and direct integration into high-performance asymmetric hollow fiber MMMs, with a focus on the resulting membrane's aging behavior and stability, is unexplored. This moves beyond powder synthesis to create a complete materials-to-device pipeline for a non-hazardously produced MOF.

proposals.title: Enhancing Olefin/Paraffin and CO2/CH4 Separation via Asymmetric Mixed-Matrix Membranes with High-Performance Nanoparticle Stabilization
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, membrane-based separation of olefin/paraffin (e.g., propylene/propane) and CO2/CH4 remains a critical industrial challenge with high energy penalties for conventional processes. While Mixed-Matrix Membranes (MMMs) show promise for CO2/N2, their performance for these more challenging, similarly sized gas pairs is less explored. Extending the proven asymmetric membrane fabrication and nanoparticle stabilization techniques from Koros's group to these systems could unlock significant process efficiency gains.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating molecular sieve nanoparticles (e.g., MOFs like ZIF-8 or SAPO-34) into an asymmetric polymer matrix via controlled pyrolysis and above-Tg annealing will create stabilized, high-performance MMMs with simultaneously enhanced selectivity and permeability for C3H6/C3H8 and CO2/CH4 gas pairs.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric MMMs using a phase-inversion process, incorporating selected nanoparticles (ZIF-8 for olefin/paraffin, amine-functionalized silicas for CO2/CH4) into polymer precursors (e.g., 6FDA-based polyimides). Membranes will undergo a controlled pyrolysis protocol, informed by prior work on Carbon Molecular Sieve membranes, followed by systematic above-Tg thermal annealing to stabilize the polymer phase and mitigate physical aging. Pure- and mixed-gas permeation tests will be conducted for C3H6/C3H8 (1:1) and CO2/CH4 (1:1) at 35°C and upstream pressures up to 10 bar, with performance benchmarked against the 2008 Robeson upper bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework for translating nanoparticle stabilization and thermal treatment strategies to industrially relevant gas pairs beyond CO2/N2. It will provide fundamental structure-property relationships for MMMs under aggressive feed conditions, contributing directly to the design of next-generation membranes for natural gas upgrading and olefin production.
proposals.novelty: The application of asymmetric, nanoparticle-stabilized MMM architectures specifically tailored for the demanding olefin/paraffin separation is novel. Combining insights from physical aging mitigation and pyrolysis stabilization for these gas pairs represents a significant, non-obvious extension of the group's prior work.

proposals.title: Asymmetric Mixed-Matrix Membranes with Ultra-High ZIF-94 Loadings: Investigating the Limits of CO2/CH4 Separation Performance and Physical Aging Stability
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) incorporating metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) like ZIF-94, which possesses intrinsic amine functionality for selective CO2 interaction, show promise for energy-efficient gas separations. However, conventional wisdom and processing limitations have historically capped MOF loadings below 40 wt%, potentially leaving significant performance gains unexplored. Pushing into ultra-high loading regimes (>40 wt%) could dramatically enhance both permeability and selectivity by maximizing the influence of the selective filler, but the resulting membrane morphology, mechanical integrity, and long-term aging behavior are unknown and critical for practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that through careful control of dope solution chemistry and asymmetric membrane fabrication techniques, ZIF-94 loadings exceeding 40 wt% can be achieved in a polyimide matrix, leading to MMMs with unprecedented CO2/CH4 selectivity and improved resistance to physical aging due to the rigid, high-loading filler network restricting polymer chain mobility.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of asymmetric, flat-sheet MMMs using a Matrimid® polyimide matrix and ZIF-94 nanoparticles at loadings of 45, 50, and 55 wt%. The dope solutions will be formulated using a solvent/non-solvent system (e.g., NMP/THF) optimized for high particle dispersion and viscosity control, informed by Koros's work on nanoparticle-stabilized membranes. Membranes will be cast via phase inversion. Performance will be evaluated using pure- and mixed-gas (CO2/CH4) permeation tests at 35°C and upstream pressures up to 10 bar. Long-term aging studies will monitor permeability and selectivity over 6-12 months.
