Morphology will be characterized by SEM, XRD, and positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) to assess particle dispersion, interface quality, and free volume.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a processing-property-performance paradigm for ultra-high-loading MMMs, defining the practical upper limits of ZIF-94 incorporation. It will provide fundamental insights into how extreme filler content alters the aging dynamics of glassy polymer matrices. The results will guide the development of next-generation membranes with simultaneously high permeability and selectivity for carbon capture and natural gas purification.
proposals.novelty: While high loadings of non-porous fillers have been studied, systematically probing loadings >40 wt% for a functional, CO2-philic MOF like ZIF-94 in an asymmetric membrane format is novel. Furthermore, explicitly linking ultra-high loading to physical aging stability—a critical industrial concern—addresses a key gap in the MMM literature.

proposals.title: Enhancing Gas Permeance via Ultrathin Mixed-Matrix Selective Layers: A Combined Interfacial Engineering and Asymmetric Fabrication Approach
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) combine the processability of polymers with the superior selectivity of molecular sieves, but their industrial application is limited by low permeance, especially for high-performance, rigid polymers. Achieving high permeance requires reducing the selective layer thickness to the sub-micron scale, which is challenging due to defects and poor nanoparticle dispersion. This work addresses the critical need for scalable fabrication of high-flux, high-selectivity composite membranes for energy-efficient gas separations like CO2/CH4 and O2/N2.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by employing a vinyltrimethoxysilane-based surface treatment of molecular sieve nanoparticles to enhance polymer-filler compatibility, followed by controlled deposition via a modified phase-inversion process, we can fabricate defect-free, ultrathin (<500 nm) MMM selective layers on macroporous supports, dramatically increasing gas permeance without sacrificing selectivity.
proposals.method: The approach will synthesize carbon molecular sieve (CMS) nanoparticles via controlled pyrolysis of a polymer precursor. These nanoparticles will be surface-modified using vinyltrimethoxysilane (inspired by recent sorbent work) to create a reactive interface. A dope solution containing a high-performance polymer (e.g., a polyimide) and the treated nanoparticles will be prepared. An asymmetric composite membrane will be fabricated using a multi-step casting and phase-inversion process, precisely controlling solvent evaporation and coagulation bath conditions to form an ultrathin, dense selective layer atop a porous support. The membranes will undergo systematic above-Tg thermal annealing to relieve stresses and optimize performance, as indicated by prior Koros group findings on aging and annealing benefits.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable fabrication methodology for high-performance ultrathin-film composite MMMs. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay between nanoparticle surface chemistry, polymer chain dynamics during thin-film formation, and final gas transport properties. The resulting membranes are expected to demonstrate a step-change improvement in gas permeance (likely >2-5x increase) compared to conventional thick MMMs, advancing the field toward practical, high-productivity membrane systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of a reactive silane coupling agent—typically used for sorbent stabilization—applied to CMS nanoparticles for MMMs, and the precise adaptation of asymmetric membrane fabrication techniques, typically for integrally-skinned membranes, to create a sub-micron composite selective layer. This moves beyond simple blending or thick film coating to a controlled, integral fabrication process.

proposals.title: Asymmetric Mixed-Matrix Membranes as High-Capacity, Pressure-Swing Modulators for Adsorptive Gas Storage and Release
proposals.motivation: Current gas storage technologies, such as high-pressure tanks or cryogenic systems, pose safety and energy penalties, while adsorbent beds can suffer from slow kinetics and poor thermal management. A membrane-based storage concept could leverage rapid, selective surface adsorption and controlled desorption via pressure or thermal swings, offering a potentially safer, more responsive system for applications like hydrogen storage or onboard carbon capture. This inverts the traditional membrane function from steady-state permeation to dynamic, cyclic sorption and release.
proposals.hypothesis: An asymmetric mixed-matrix membrane (MMM), with a highly porous support and a thin, nanoparticle-stabilized selective layer rich in microporous fillers (e.g., MOFs, carbon molecular sieves), can function as a high-surface-area, mechanically robust 'membrane sorbent' capable of storing significant gas quantities via adsorption and enabling rapid, controlled release through applied transmembrane pressure or thermal gradients.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric MMMs via phase inversion, incorporating SBA-15 or ZIF-8 nanoparticles into a polymer matrix (e.g., Matrimid®), followed by controlled pyrolysis or silane treatment to enhance stability and microporosity, as inspired by Koros's recent work. The 'inverse' performance will be characterized not by permeability/selectivity but by high-pressure adsorption isotherms (e.g., H2, CO2, CH4) and dynamic pressure-swing desorption cycles using a modified volumetric apparatus. Key metrics will include working capacity, adsorption kinetics, and cyclic stability over hundreds of pressure-swing operations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new framework for 'membrane sorbents,' defining performance metrics distinct from traditional separation membranes. It will provide fundamental data on the coupling of adsorption thermodynamics with the constrained transport environment of an asymmetric MMM, potentially leading to a new class of compact, modular gas storage/delivery devices.
proposals.novelty: While MMMs are extensively studied for separation, their deliberate design and evaluation as cyclic, high-capacity gas storage media is a fundamentally non-obvious inversion of their typical function, merging concepts from adsorbent design with membrane morphology control to address kinetic limitations in conventional sorbents.

proposals.title: High-Performance Mixed-Matrix Membranes of ZIF-94 in 6FDA-DAM for Organic Solvent Nanofiltration: Mitigating Aging and Plasticization
proposals.motivation: Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) is critical for energy-efficient chemical separations, but polymeric membranes suffer from performance decay due to physical aging and solvent-induced plasticization. Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) offer a promising route to enhance stability and selectivity, yet their application in demanding liquid environments remains underexplored. This work addresses the need for robust, high-flux membranes for solvent-intensive processes like pharmaceutical purification.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating ZIF-94 nanoparticles into the high-free-volume polymer 6FDA-DAM will create a hybrid matrix that simultaneously suppresses physical aging of the polymer and provides molecular-sieving pathways, leading to MMMs with superior and stable OSN performance under aggressive solvent feeds.
proposals.method: We will synthesize ZIF-94 nanoparticles and fabricate asymmetric MMMs via phase inversion, using pre-2026 protocols for 6FDA-DAM dope solutions. Membrane performance will be evaluated for solvent permeance and solute rejection (e.g., dyes, pharmaceuticals) in solvents like methanol and acetone. Key analyses will include long-term aging studies, positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) to probe free volume evolution, and sorption/swelling tests to quantify plasticization resistance. The physical aging behavior and solvent stability will be compared to pristine 6FDA-DAM controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-property relationship for ZIF-94/6FDA-DAM MMMs in liquid separations, quantifying their anti-aging and anti-plasticization benefits. It will provide a foundational framework for designing next-generation OSN membranes that leverage the synergy between rigid molecular sieves and high-performance polymers, moving beyond gas separation applications.
proposals.novelty: While ZIF-94 and 6FDA-DAM have been studied for gas separation, their combined use in OSN to combat both physical aging and plasticization—two primary degradation mechanisms in liquid systems—is a novel and non-obvious application of the MMM concept, directly extending Koros's expertise in aging and asymmetric structures to liquid-phase challenges.

proposals.title: Resolving the Driving Force in Glassy Polymer Membranes: A Systematic Comparison of Chemical Potential Gradient and Concentration Gradient Formulations for Gas Transport
proposals.motivation: The fundamental description of gas transport in glassy polymer membranes remains contested. While the solution-diffusion model traditionally uses a concentration gradient, arguments based on non-equilibrium thermodynamics favor the chemical potential gradient as the true driving force. This ambiguity impedes the accurate prediction of membrane performance, especially for complex, asymmetric structures and under high-pressure or mixed-gas conditions relevant to carbon capture and hydrogen purification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the chemical potential gradient formulation will provide a superior, more physically consistent fit to experimental gas permeation data across a range of glassy polymers and penetrant gases, particularly for systems exhibiting strong non-ideality, such as highly sorbing gases (e.g., CO2) or plasticizing polymers.
proposals.method: We will conduct systematic pure- and mixed-gas permeation experiments using a high-pressure, variable-temperature apparatus on a suite of well-characterized glassy polymers (e.g., Matrimid, 6FDA-based polyimides, thermally rearranged polymers) and gases (He, H2, O2, N2, CH4, CO2). Sorption isotherms will be measured independently using a microbalance. Transport data will be fitted using two models: 1) the conventional Fickian formulation with concentration-dependent diffusivity, and 2) a chemical potential gradient formulation, where flux is proportional to the gradient in penetrant fugacity. The consistency of fitted parameters (e.g., diffusivity prefactor, activation energy) and predictive accuracy for mixed-gas performance will be rigorously compared using statistical metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, data-driven assessment of the most appropriate fundamental transport formulation for glassy polymers. It will yield a clearer physical framework for modeling advanced asymmetric and nanocomposite membranes, directly informing the design of next-generation separation materials. The results will resolve a long-standing theoretical debate with practical implications for process simulation and membrane optimization.
proposals.novelty: While the theoretical basis for each formulation is known, a comprehensive, experimental model comparison across diverse polymer-gas systems, explicitly linking to mixed-gas performance prediction, has not been systematically performed within the membrane community.

proposals.title: Extending Partial Immobilization Theory to Model Mixed-Gas Transport in Asymmetric Thin-Film Composite Membranes
proposals.motivation: Thin-film composite (TFC) membranes are the industrial standard for gas separations, but their performance is often predicted using models for homogeneous films, neglecting the complex interplay between the selective layer and the porous support. This gap limits the rational design of next-generation membranes for carbon capture and hydrogen purification. A robust, physics-based model for gas permeation in asymmetric structures is critically needed to deconvolute intrinsic material properties from structural effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the partial immobilization theory, which successfully describes sorption and diffusion in mixed-matrix and homogeneous glassy polymers, can be extended to model mixed-gas transport in asymmetric TFC membranes by explicitly accounting for the resistance and potential sorption capacity of the porous support layer.
proposals.method: We will fabricate model TFC membranes with systematically varied polyimide-based selective layer thicknesses on asymmetric polysulfone supports. Pure- and mixed-gas (e.g., CO2/CH4) permeation experiments will be conducted. The partial immobilization model will be adapted by treating the membrane as a two-layer composite: a dense surface layer governed by dual-mode sorption and mobility parameters, and a porous sublayer modeled as a low-density, partially immobilizing region with enhanced free volume. Numerical fitting of transient and steady-state permeation data will extract intrinsic material parameters for the selective layer, independent of support effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new predictive framework for gas transport in industrially relevant asymmetric membranes, moving beyond idealized film models. It will enable researchers to accurately extract the true performance of novel selective layers from composite membrane data, accelerating materials discovery. The model will also offer insights into the role of support-layer aging and physical conditioning on overall membrane performance.
proposals.novelty: While partial immobilization theory is established for homogeneous films, its application to deconvolute transport in asymmetric TFC structures, where the support is treated as a sorbing, resistive layer with distinct morphological parameters, is novel. This approach directly addresses the scaling challenge from ideal dense films to practical composite membranes.

proposals.title: Quantifying and Mitigating Physical Aging in High-Performance Mixed-Matrix Membranes for CO2/CH4 Separation
proposals.motivation: Physical aging in glassy polymer membranes, driven by the slow relaxation of non-equilibrium free volume, leads to a well-documented decline in permeability over time, compromising long-term separation performance and economic viability. While organic-inorganic composite (mixed-matrix) membranes offer enhanced initial performance, the long-term aging behavior of these complex, asymmetric materials is poorly understood, creating a critical barrier to their industrial deployment for gas separations like natural gas purification.
proposals.hypothesis: The incorporation of judiciously selected, surface-modified inorganic nanoparticles into high-free-volume glassy polymers will not only enhance initial selectivity but will also significantly retard the rate of physical aging by imposing topological constraints on polymer chain segmental mobility, thereby stabilizing long-term gas transport properties.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of asymmetric flat-sheet mixed-matrix membranes using a high-free-volume polymer (e.g., 6FDA-DAM) and nanoparticles (e.g., SBA-15 silica) treated with vinyltrimethoxysilane to ensure compatibility. Membranes will be subjected to accelerated aging at elevated temperatures (e.g., 35-65°C) under vacuum and under mixed-gas (CO2/CH4) feed pressure. Long-term performance will be tracked via periodic pure- and mixed-gas permeation tests. The physical aging kinetics will be quantified by monitoring the time-dependent decay in permeability, analyzed using established free volume models (e.g., the Kohlrausch-Williams-Watts stretched exponential function) and correlated with detailed characterization (XRD, positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy, DMA) of structural evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative dataset on the physical aging kinetics of high-performance mixed-matrix membranes, directly linking nanoparticle interface engineering to aging resistance. It will establish design rules for creating composite membranes with not only high initial performance but also long-term stability, a crucial step toward reliable industrial implementation.
proposals.novelty: While aging in pure polymers is studied, systematically quantifying how nanoparticle addition and surface chemistry alter the physical aging trajectory in asymmetric composite membranes is novel. The approach combines Koros group's expertise in accelerated aging protocols, asymmetric membrane formation, and interface modification to address a fundamental stability challenge.

proposals.title: Robotic Synthesis and High-Throughput Characterization for Mapping Concentration-Dependent Transport Mechanisms in Polymer Membrane Libraries
proposals.motivation: The performance of polymer membranes for gas separations and controlled release is governed by complex, concentration-dependent sorption and diffusion phenomena. Current material discovery is slow and relies on sparse data, failing to capture the full transport landscape needed to design materials for variable feed conditions, such as in post-combustion CO2 capture or olefin/paraffin separation. A systematic, high-throughput approach to map these nonlinear behaviors is required to move beyond empirical selection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating robotic synthesis with high-throughput, variable-activity permeation testing will enable the construction of quantitative 'transport mechanism maps' that directly link specific polymer chemical motifs to their characteristic concentration-dependent diffusion parameters, revealing design rules for targeted performance under real-world, non-ideal conditions.
proposals.method: An automated platform will synthesize a diverse library of glassy and rubbery polymers via robotic controlled polymerization. For each polymer, a high-throughput variable-pressure/time-lag system will collect dense permeability datasets for key gases (e.g., CO2, CH4, O2, N2) across a wide range of upstream activities. Complementary automated gravimetric sorption analysis will provide concurrent sorption isotherms. The high-dimensional data will be fitted to advanced diffusion models, such as the dual-mode sorption/partial immobilization model for glassy polymers and free-volume models (e.g., Vrentas-Duda) for rubbers, using custom Bayesian optimization algorithms to extract concentration-dependent diffusion coefficients and thermodynamic parameters for thousands of material-penetrant pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for accelerated discovery of membrane materials by generating the first extensive, publicly accessible database linking polymer chemistry to full-concentration-range transport parameters. It will yield fundamental insights into how specific functional groups and chain architectures modulate penetrant mobility, directly informing the rational design of next-generation membranes for challenging separations where plasticization or competitive sorption is critical.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop synergy of robotic synthesis, high-throughput characterization at non-constant penetrant activity, and automated fitting of advanced physical models on an unprecedented scale, moving from discrete material screening to the continuous mapping of transport mechanism landscapes.

proposals.title: Mapping and Engineering Free Volume Chemistry for Enhanced Solubility-Selective Gas Separations in Asymmetric Polymer Membranes
proposals.motivation: Membrane-based gas separations for CO2/CH4 and olefin/paraffin are often limited by the traditional focus on diffusivity selectivity, which can lead to performance trade-offs. Solubility selectivity offers a promising alternative, but its rational design is hindered by a lack of tools to characterize and correlate the specific chemical nature and size distribution of free volume elements with sorption thermodynamics. Pre-2026, PALS has been used primarily to measure average free volume hole sizes, not their chemical environment or distribution.
proposals.hypothesis: By using PALS with chemically sensitive positron probes (e.g., o-Ps quenching analysis) and correlating results with detailed sorption isotherms, we can establish quantitative structure-property relationships linking engineered free volume architectures—defined by size distribution and local chemistry—to enhanced solubility selectivity in asymmetric polymer membranes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of asymmetric membranes from polymers with systematically varied backbone stiffness and pendant functional groups (e.g., polar moieties like those in VTMS-treated materials). PALS will be employed not just for average hole size, but to deconvolute free volume element distributions and probe local polarity via o-Ps lifetime intensity changes. This PALS-guided characterization will be directly correlated with high-pressure CO2 and CH4 (or C2H4/C2H6) sorption isotherms and mixed-gas permeation tests. The physical aging behavior of these engineered structures will be monitored to assess stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new materials-by-design framework that inverts the traditional membrane development paradigm by prioritizing solubility control. It will provide the first quantitative relationships between PALS-measured free volume chemistry/distribution and gas solubility selectivity, enabling the targeted synthesis of next-generation membranes. The outcome will be advanced asymmetric membranes with demonstrably improved performance for challenging solubility-driven separations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using PALS as a guide to engineer the *chemical nature* of free volume, moving beyond average size metrics to create solubility-selective architectures, a significant departure from Koros's prior work on diffusion-controlled carbon molecular sieve and physical aging-stabilized membranes.

proposals.title: Engineering High Solubility-Selectivity Membranes via Asymmetric Polymer Blends for Enhanced CO2/CH4 Separation
proposals.motivation: Conventional carbon molecular sieve (CMS) and polymer membranes for gas separations like CO2/CH4 primarily exploit diffusivity selectivity, which often leads to a well-known permeability-selectivity trade-off (Robeson upper bound). For gas pairs with significant condensability differences, solubility selectivity offers a theoretically powerful but underexploited pathway to surpass this bound. Prioritizing solubility-driven separation could enable more efficient natural gas upgrading and carbon capture, addressing critical pre-2026 energy and environmental challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing asymmetric, blend-based membrane precursors with tailored free volume and specific interaction sites, we can create pyrolyzed CMS or hybrid membranes where solubility selectivity for CO2 over CH4 is the dominant separation mechanism, leading to a simultaneous increase in both permeability and selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize asymmetric blend membranes using high-performance polymers (e.g., Matrimid) with solubility-enhancing additives like vinyltrimethoxysilane (VTMS)-treated nanoparticles or tailored polymers. Precise control of blend composition and casting conditions will create an asymmetric structure with a thin, solubility-selective active layer. The pyrolysis protocol will be optimized using above-Tg annealing steps to stabilize a nanoporous architecture that preserves specific sorption sites (e.g., polarizable or quadrupole-interacting domains) while controlling ultramicropore size. Gas permeation (CO2, CH4, N2) and detailed sorption isotherm measurements will be used to deconvolute solubility and diffusivity contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new design framework for moving beyond the diffusivity-selectivity paradigm, demonstrating a material platform where solubility is engineered to dominate. It will yield fundamental structure-property relationships for solubility-selective CMS/hybrid membranes and deliver experimental gas separation performance data that challenges the conventional upper bound for the CO2/CH4 pair.
proposals.novelty: While Koros's prior work used annealing to improve diffusivity selectivity in CMS, this proposal inverts the priority to deliberately design and stabilize solubility-selective domains within an asymmetric, pyrolyzed matrix—a non-obvious target given the historical focus on molecular sieving. The use of VTMS-modified additives to create stable, specific interaction sites post-pyrolysis is a novel approach to enhance solubility.

proposals.title: A Kinetic Framework for Quantifying Plasticization-Induced Selectivity Loss in Polymer Membranes Using Enzyme Inhibition Analogies
proposals.motivation: Plasticization by condensable penetrants like CO2 remains a critical barrier to the long-term stability of high-performance glassy polymer membranes for gas separations. Current understanding is largely empirical, correlating swelling with selectivity loss, but lacks a predictive, mechanistic model for how the local penetrant environment alters the matrix's intrinsic selectivity. A quantitative framework is needed to guide the design of plasticization-resistant materials and processes.
proposals.hypothesis: The plasticizing effect of a penetrant on a polymer matrix can be quantitatively described using formalisms analogous to mixed inhibition in enzyme kinetics, where the penetrant acts as a co-solvent that alters the apparent 'rate constants' (permeability coefficients) for competing gas species, enabling the prediction of selectivity as a function of penetrant activity.
proposals.method: We will measure time-dependent permeability of gas pairs (e.g., CO2/CH4) in asymmetric hollow fiber membranes (e.g., Matrimid® and 6FDA-based polyimides) under controlled upstream activities of a plasticizing vapor (e.g., CO2, propane). Data will be fitted to a modified kinetic model, treating the polymer's free volume elements as 'active sites' and the plasticizer as a modifier. The model will yield parameters analogous to competitive and non-competitive inhibition constants, quantifying the impact on sorption (affinity) and diffusion (turnover) selectivity components. This analysis will be complemented by in-situ gravimetric sorption and positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) to correlate kinetic parameters with physical aging and free volume changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, quantitative language for describing and predicting plasticization phenomena, moving beyond qualitative swelling observations. It will yield a set of measurable kinetic parameters that can link membrane performance to underlying polymer-penetrant interactions, informing the development of next-generation materials and conditioning protocols. The framework will be applicable to both rubbery and glassy polymers, offering a unified analysis tool.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of rigorous biochemical kinetic models, a formalism not previously applied to membrane plasticization, to deconvolute the effects of a penetrant on sorption and diffusion selectivity. This provides a mechanistic alternative to purely phenomenological correlations.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Temporal Evolution of Plasticization-Induced Selectivity in Glassy and Rubbery Polymer Membranes for CO2/CH4 Separation
proposals.motivation: Plasticization by aggressive penetrants like CO2 is a critical, performance-limiting phenomenon in polymer membranes, causing time-dependent swelling and a well-documented loss of selectivity. However, a systematic, quantitative framework linking the kinetics and magnitude of plasticization to the resulting evolution of mixed-gas selectivity for both rubbery and glassy polymers is lacking. This gap hinders the rational design of membranes with stable performance for natural gas purification and carbon capture, where long-term stability is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal evolution of selectivity under plasticizing conditions is governed by distinct, quantifiable physical aging and swelling mechanisms in glassy versus rubbery polymers, and that these mechanisms can be decoupled and modeled to predict long-term performance.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric hollow fiber membranes from a model glassy polymer (e.g., 6FDA-based polyimide) and a model rubbery polymer (e.g., polydimethylsiloxane). Membranes will be subjected to long-term, high-pressure mixed-gas (CO2/CH4) exposure. We will employ in-situ gravimetric sorption (to measure penetrant-induced dilation) and dynamic mechanical analysis to track physical property changes. Mixed-gas permeation tests will be conducted at regular intervals to correlate time-dependent selectivity with physical aging (for glassy polymers) and equilibrium swelling (for rubbery polymers) parameters, using established free volume and dual-mode sorption models for analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative correlation between the kinetics of plasticization-driven physical changes and the resulting selectivity trajectory for distinct polymer classes. It will yield a predictive framework to assess membrane stability, directly informing the selection and modification of polymers for aggressive gas separations. The findings will advance strategies for mitigating performance decay, such as cross-linking or annealing, by clarifying their mechanistic impact on the plasticization process.
proposals.novelty: While plasticization is known to reduce selectivity, this study uniquely focuses on quantifying the *rate* and *pathway* of this decline by decoupling the competing effects of swelling and physical aging over time, specifically contrasting the fundamental responses of rubbery and glassy polymers under identical mixed-gas conditions.

proposals.title: Decoupling Cavity Rigidity and Matrix Ductility: Synthesis of Spirobifluorene-Based Polyimides with Oligosiloxane Spacers for Enhanced Gas Separation Membranes
proposals.motivation: Advanced polymer membranes for gas separations like CO2/CH4 or O2/N2 face a persistent trade-off: highly rigid, contorted polymers (e.g., polymers of intrinsic microporosity, PIMs) offer exceptional size-sieving selectivity but suffer from physical aging, brittleness, and plasticization under aggressive feeds. Conversely, more flexible polymers are mechanically robust but lack the required selectivity. A material design strategy that decouples the rigid, selective sites from the matrix's mechanical properties is critically needed for durable, high-performance membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthesizing polymers where shape-persistent, contorted spirobifluorene units are linked via flexible, hydrolytically stable oligodimethylsiloxane spacers will create a membrane material that maintains high size-sieving selectivity from the rigid cavities while exhibiting superior mechanical integrity, reduced physical aging, and enhanced resistance to CO2-induced plasticization compared to state-of-the-art PIMs.
proposals.method: A series of novel poly(imide-siloxane) copolymers will be synthesized via polycondensation of a contorted, spiro-centric dianhydride with amine-terminated oligodimethylsiloxanes of varying chain length (n=3, 5, 10). The synthesis will be followed by solution casting to form asymmetric, integrally skinned membranes. Membrane performance will be characterized by pure- and mixed-gas permeation tests for CO2/CH4 and O2/N2 pairs. Physical aging will be monitored via long-term permeability decay studies, and plasticization resistance will be assessed with high-pressure CO2 feeds. Material properties will be evaluated using techniques like positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) for cavity size distribution, and dynamic mechanical analysis for mechanical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for separation membranes by demonstrating that selectivity and mechanical robustness can be independently tuned. It will yield a fundamental understanding of how flexible spacers influence the packing and stability of rigid, contorted units. The resulting polymer family is expected to provide a tangible advancement towards membranes with combined high performance and long-term operational stability for carbon capture and other gas separations.
proposals.novelty: While siloxanes are known for flexibility and PIMs for rigidity, the deliberate molecular-level decoupling of a contorted site from the polymer backbone via a stable, flexible spacer to simultaneously address aging, processability, and plasticization is a novel and non-obvious integration of concepts from Koros's work on aging and asymmetric membranes with polymer design.

proposals.title: In-Silico Design of Kinked Polymer Architectures for Selective Ethylene/Ethane Transport via Free Energy Pathway Mapping
proposals.motivation: The energy-intensive separation of ethylene from ethane via cryogenic distillation remains a major industrial challenge. Polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) and other contorted glassy polymers offer high permeability but often suffer from selectivity trade-offs and physical aging, which reduces performance over time. A predictive design framework is needed to create polymers with rigid, non-equilibrium free volume elements that provide both high selectivity and long-term stability for this critical separation.
proposals.hypothesis: That molecular dynamics simulations, guided by transition state theory, can be used to design specific, synthetically accessible kinks and contortions in polymer backbones to create programmed, high-energy-barrier diffusion pathways, thereby maximizing ethylene/ethane selectivity while mitigating aging through inherent rigidity.
proposals.method: We will employ all-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using GROMACS to model candidate contorted monomers (e.g., spirobisindane, triptycene variants with programmed kinks). The simulated polymer matrices will be analyzed using cavity detection algorithms (e.g., Zeo++) to map free volume topology. Metadynamics and umbrella sampling will be used to calculate the free energy barriers and diffusion pathways for ethylene and ethane, explicitly modeling the hopping mechanism. The most promising in-silico architectures will be synthesized via polycondensation, cast into asymmetric membranes, and characterized for gas separation performance and physical aging resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated computational pipeline for the rational design of contorted polymers, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will produce new, high-performance membrane materials with demonstrated ethylene/ethane separation capability and provide fundamental insights into the relationship between programmed backbone contortion, free energy landscapes for transport, and long-term stability.
proposals.novelty: The approach directly integrates advanced MD techniques from biophysics (free energy calculation) into the polymer design loop for separations, focusing on engineering specific diffusion *pathways* rather than just total free volume, and explicitly targets aging mitigation through designed rigidity from the outset.

proposals.title: Enhancing Aging Resistance and Performance of Nanoparticle-Stabilized CMS Membranes via Tailored Asymmetric Support Structures
proposals.motivation: Carbon molecular sieve (CMS) membranes derived from polymer precursors offer exceptional gas separation performance but are susceptible to physical aging, which reduces permeability over time. While nanoparticle stabilization and above-Tg annealing have shown promise in mitigating aging, these selective layers are often fragile and require robust support. Developing a composite architecture that synergistically combines a stabilized selective layer with an optimized porous substrate is critical for creating durable, high-performance membranes for energy-intensive gas separations like CO2/CH4.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an asymmetric, high-porosity substrate with tailored surface chemistry and pore morphology will provide superior mechanical stability and interfacial adhesion for nanoparticle-stabilized CMS selective layers, thereby preserving their high permeability and selectivity while significantly enhancing their resistance to physical aging and mechanical failure.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric polyimide supports via phase inversion, controlling porosity and surface pore size via casting parameters. The support surface will be modified using vapor-phase treatments (e.g., inspired by vinyltrimethoxysilane chemistry) to enhance compatibility. Nanoparticle-stabilized polymer precursors (e.g., Matrimid with dispersed non-porous silica) will be coated as a thin selective layer via dip-coating. The composite will be pyrolyzed under controlled conditions, with subsequent above-Tg annealing. Performance will be evaluated for CO2/CH4 separation, with long-term aging studies monitoring permeability and selectivity. Physical characterization (SEM, XPS, porosimetry) will link structure to performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework for designing next-generation composite CMS membranes with decoupled optimization of selective layer chemistry and substrate mechanics. It will provide fundamental insights into how substrate properties influence the aging behavior and integrity of ultra-thin, stabilized CMS films, moving beyond the current focus solely on the selective layer material.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the intentional integration of pre-2026 Koros group advancements—nanoparticle stabilization, above-Tg annealing, and chemical treatment—into a single composite membrane architecture where the asymmetric support is not passive but actively engineered to combat the primary failure modes (aging, delamination) of high-performance CMS layers.

proposals.title: Amphiphilic Block Copolymer Compatibilizers for Homogeneous Mixed-Matrix Membranes: A Nanoemulsion-Inspired Strategy to Mitigate Filler Aggregation and Interface Defects
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) incorporating nanoporous fillers like MOFs or zeolites offer a promising route to transcend the polymer performance upper bound for gas separations. However, their practical realization is hindered by poor filler dispersion and weak polymer-filler interfaces, leading to non-selective defects and compromised mechanical integrity. Drawing an analogy to the stabilization of immiscible phases in pharmaceutical nanoemulsions provides a novel conceptual framework to address this long-standing compatibility challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: That custom-designed amphiphilic block copolymers, with one block tailored to adsorb onto the filler surface and the other engineered to be compatible with a high-performance polymer matrix, will act as effective steric compatibilizers. This will suppress filler aggregation, minimize interfacial defects, and lead to MMMs with enhanced gas separation performance and physical aging resistance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of block copolymers (e.g., poly(ethylene oxide)-b-poly(styrene) derivatives) where the anchor block is functionalized with groups (e.g., phosphonates, catechols) known for strong binding to target fillers like ZIF-8. The matrix-compatible block will be matched to a high-performance polyimide (e.g., 6FDA-DAM). Fillers will be pre-treated with these copolymers via solution-phase grafting. Asymmetric MMMs will be fabricated via a standard phase-inversion process. Characterization will include TEM for dispersion analysis, XPS for surface chemistry, and detailed gas permeation (CO2/CH4, O2/N2) testing before and after accelerated physical aging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a systematic, bio-inspired design principle for MMM compatibilization, moving beyond trial-and-error additive use. It will deliver a concrete materials platform—surface-grafted, sterically stabilized fillers—that directly addresses dispersion and interface issues, potentially enabling the reliable fabrication of high-filler-loading MMMs with predictable and stable performance.
proposals.novelty: While block copolymers have been studied as membrane materials, their deliberate design as steric stabilizers to compatibilize two disparate phases in an MMM, directly inspired by pharmaceutical nanoemulsion technology, is a novel and unexplored approach. This shifts the focus from modifying the bulk matrix to engineering the filler surface with tailored macromolecular agents.

proposals.title: Fabrication of Ultrathin, Defect-Free Polymer Selective Layers via Langmuir-Blodgett Assembly for Enhanced Gas Separation Performance
proposals.motivation: The performance of gas separation membranes is governed by the trade-off between permeance and selectivity, where ultrathin, defect-free selective layers are critical. Conventional methods like interfacial polymerization or coating often struggle to produce sub-100-nm layers with perfect integrity and controlled molecular ordering, leading to performance compromises. The Langmuir-Blodgett (LB) technique, a pre-2026 method from colloid science, offers a potential solution by enabling precise, layer-by-layer assembly of monolayers with controlled packing and orientation at an interface.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Langmuir-Blodgett technique can be adapted to fabricate ultrathin (<50 nm), pinhole-free selective layers from specially designed glassy polymers or polymer precursors, and that the precise molecular packing achieved at the air-water interface will translate to enhanced gas pair selectivity (e.g., CO2/CH4, O2/N2) without sacrificing permeance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize amphiphilic polyimide precursors or functionalized polymers capable of forming stable Langmuir monolayers at an air-water interface. Using a Langmuir-Blodgett trough, we will compress these monolayers to a target surface pressure, characterized by in-situ surface pressure-area isotherms and Brewster angle microscopy, to achieve a tightly packed, ordered state. This monolayer will then be transferred layer-by-layer onto asymmetric, porous polymeric supports (e.g., Matrimid® or P84 polyimide). The transferred layers will be thermally treated or cross-linked (e.g., via above-Tg annealing, drawing from recent group work) to stabilize the structure and finalize the selective skin. Membrane performance will be evaluated using pure and mixed gas permeation tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel fabrication pathway for next-generation composite membranes with record-thin, ordered selective skins, directly addressing the permeance-selectivity trade-off. It will provide fundamental insights into the relationship between 2D interfacial packing of polymer chains at an air-water interface and their resulting 3D gas transport properties. The successful adaptation of the LB technique could open a new design space for membrane materials beyond conventional solution-processing limits.
proposals.novelty: While LB films are well-known for lipids and nanoparticles, their application to create ultrathin, defect-free selective layers from high-performance glassy polymers for gas separation is novel and non-obvious. This approach merges the precise interfacial control of colloid science with the demanding performance requirements of industrial membrane separations, a synergy not yet fully explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of High-Performance Polymers for Target Gas Separations via a Hybrid Machine Learning and Molecular Simulation Framework
proposals.motivation: The design of polymer membranes for gas separations, such as CO2/CH4 or O2/N2, remains a trial-and-error process reliant on synthesizing and testing numerous candidates. This is inefficient and fails to systematically explore the vast chemical space of possible polymer structures. A computational inverse design approach, which starts from a target performance profile (e.g., Robeson upper bound permeability/selectivity pairs) and identifies optimal polymer chemistries, would dramatically accelerate the discovery of next-generation membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid framework combining generative machine learning and high-throughput molecular simulation can efficiently map the relationship between polymer repeat unit structure and gas transport properties, enabling the inverse design of novel polymer candidates that meet or exceed pre-specified gas separation targets.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational autoencoder (VAE) to generate novel polymer repeat unit structures in a continuous latent space. This will be coupled with a forward-prediction surrogate model, likely a graph neural network (GNN), trained on a large dataset of polymer structures and their gas permeability coefficients (e.g., for CO2, CH4, N2, O2) computed via high-throughput molecular dynamics and Monte Carlo simulations (using tools like LAMMPS and RASPA). An iterative optimization loop (e.g., using Bayesian optimization) will then search the VAE's latent space to minimize the difference between the surrogate model's predictions and the target gas separation performance, thereby generating candidate structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational pipeline for the *de novo* design of polymer membranes, moving beyond quantitative structure-property relationship (QSPR) models to active generative design. It will yield a shortlist of promising, non-intuitive polymer chemistries for synthesis and testing, validated against Koros group expertise in physical aging and asymmetric membrane formation. The open-source framework will provide the community with a powerful tool for targeted materials discovery.
proposals.novelty: While forward-prediction models exist, inverting the design problem for gas separation polymers using a generative deep learning approach is largely unexplored. The integration of a VAE for chemical structure generation with physics-based simulation data and performance-targeted optimization represents a significant departure from conventional heuristic or database-screening methods.

proposals.title: Decoupling Competitive Sorption and Plasticization Effects in Asymmetric Carbon Molecular Sieve Membranes Under Realistic Mixed-Gas Conditions
proposals.motivation: Membrane performance for gas separations like CO2/CH4 is typically evaluated using pure-gas permeation data, which fails to predict behavior under realistic multicomponent feeds. Competitive sorption and plasticization effects in complex mixtures can drastically alter selectivity and permeability, leading to suboptimal or failed process design. A predictive framework for mixed-gas performance is therefore critical for translating advanced materials, such as nanoparticle-stabilized carbon molecular sieve (CMS) membranes, from the lab to industrial applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mixed-gas separation performance of asymmetric CMS membranes can be quantitatively predicted by a coupled model that independently accounts for competitive sorption-induced mobility changes and pressure-dependent plasticization, parameters which can be extracted from a limited set of targeted pure- and mixed-gas experiments.
proposals.method: We will fabricate asymmetric CMS membranes via pyrolysis of a polymer precursor, employing above-Tg annealing protocols to stabilize nanostructure. High-pressure mixed-gas permeation experiments for CO2/CH4/N2 mixtures will be conducted using a custom-built apparatus. Data will be modeled using a modified dual-mode sorption-mobility framework, incorporating the Non-Equilibrium Lattice Fluid (NELF) model for competitive sorption and a concentration-dependent diffusivity matrix to capture coupling effects. Model parameters will be regressed from pure-gas isotherms and transient permeation data, then validated against independent mixed-gas tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, mechanistic model for predicting mixed-gas performance in asymmetric CMS membranes, directly addressing a major scale-up bottleneck. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay of physical aging, competitive sorption, and plasticization under process-relevant conditions. The results will establish guidelines for tailoring membrane stabilization protocols (e.g., annealing, crosslinking) for optimal mixed-gas performance.
proposals.novelty: While mixed-gas studies exist, the novelty lies in decoupling and quantitatively modeling the distinct contributions of competitive sorption and plasticization within the complex, non-equilibrium nanostructure of asymmetric CMS membranes, moving beyond empirical correlations or pure-gas extrapolations.

ideas.title: PALS-Guided Design of Membranes with Engineered Solubility-Selective Free Volume
ideas.description: This research direction combines the goal of prioritizing solubility selectivity in membrane design with the advanced characterization tool of Positron Annihilation Lifetime Spectroscopy (PALS). The core synergy is using PALS to move beyond measuring average free volume to mapping the size distribution and chemical nature of free volume elements that preferentially interact with and solubilize one gas over another. This allows for the creation of structure-property relationships that directly link specific free volume architectures—such as tailored cavity sizes with specific functional group chemistries—to enhanced solubility selectivity for target gas pairs like CO2/CH4 or olefin/paraffin. The outcome is a materials-by-design framework where PALS data informs the synthesis of next-generation polymers with free volume engineered for sorption-based separation, fundamentally inverting the traditional reliance on diffusivity control.

ideas.title: Synthesize polymers with shape-persistent contorted units linked by flexible, stable spacers for robust size-sieving membranes
ideas.description: This approach designs polymers that integrate rigid, contorted molecular building blocks to create shape-persistent, size-selective cavities within the polymer matrix. These kinked units are connected not directly to each other, but through chemically robust, flexible linker segments. The linkers decouple the contorted sites, mitigating brittleness and enabling solution processability into durable films. The resulting membrane material maintains high size-sieving selectivity due to the rigid cavities while exhibiting the mechanical integrity and plasticization resistance required for practical gas or liquid separation applications.

ideas.title: Computational Design of Contorted Polymers for Programmed Molecular Transport
ideas.description: This research direction integrates molecular dynamics (MD) simulations from biophysics directly into the design loop for synthesizing polymers with highly rigid, contorted backbones. Instead of synthesizing polymers first and then characterizing their transport properties, the approach uses MD to simulate and map the free volume topology and energy barriers created by specific, designed kinks and contortions in a polymer chain. This allows for the in-silico screening and optimization of monomer structures to create matrices with programmed 'hopping' pathways that maximize selectivity for challenging gas separations (e.g., ethylene/ethane). The validated computational models then guide the targeted synthesis of only the most promising, pre-simulated contorted architectures, dramatically accelerating the discovery of next-generation separation membranes.

ideas.title: Adopt amphiphilic block copolymer strategies from pharmaceutical nanoemulsions for mixed-matrix membrane compatibilization
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from pharmaceutical nanotechnology, where amphiphilic block copolymers are used to stabilize immiscible phases in nanoemulsions. The analogous problem of preventing phase separation and aggregation in drug delivery systems is solved by using steric stabilizers like Pluronics, which position themselves at the oil-water interface. Adapted to mixed-matrix membranes, the research involves designing custom amphiphilic block copolymers with one block tailored to bind to the filler surface (e.g., metal-organic frameworks, zeolites) and the other block engineered to be compatible with the continuous polymer matrix (e.g., polyimide, polysulfone). These compatibilizers will be pre-grafted onto fillers to create a protective, steric layer that ensures homogeneous dispersion during membrane casting, minimizes defect formation at the polymer-filler interface, and improves the mechanical integrity and separation performance of the resulting membrane.

ideas.title: Quantifying the 'Solvent Effect' on Polymer Membrane Selectivity Using Enzyme Kinetics Frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts solution frameworks from enzyme biochemistry, where kinetic models are used to quantify how solvent environments alter enzymatic selectivity. The analogous problem is the systematic study of solvent effects on enzyme selectivity, solved by measuring changes in kinetic parameters under varying conditions. Here, we treat the plasticizing penetrant in a polymer membrane as an analogous 'co-solvent' altering the matrix's 'active site' for permeation. The adapted idea is to quantify plasticization effects on membrane selectivity by applying kinetic formalism: measuring time-dependent permeability of gas pairs under controlled penetrant activities and fitting the data to models that parameterize the plasticization impact on diffusion and sorption 'rate constants.' This provides a predictive, mechanistic framework for selectivity loss in both rubbery and glassy polymers, moving beyond empirical observation.

ideas.title: Langmuir-Blodgett Assembly of Ultrathin Selective Membranes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from colloid and interface science—specifically the Langmuir-Blodgett (LB) technique for creating uniform molecular films—to the challenge of fabricating defect-free, sub-100-nm selective layers on porous supports. The analogous problem of creating perfectly ordered, ultrathin films at an interface was solved by compressing a monolayer of molecules to a specific surface pressure and transferring it layer-by-layer onto a substrate. The adapted research idea proposes using the LB technique to assemble selective layers from designed polymers or 2D material nanosheets at an air-water interface. By precisely controlling the compression and transfer process, this method aims to deposit a highly ordered, pinhole-free selective skin directly onto a support, thereby maximizing permeance through minimal thickness while maintaining selectivity through perfect layer integrity and molecular ordering.

ideas.title: Investigate MOPs as 'Molecular Rebar' for Anti-Aging Mixed Matrix Membranes
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from civil engineering's use of rebar in concrete to the problem of physical aging in Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMMs). The analogous field is Civil/Structural Engineering and Composite Materials, where a steel rebar grid is embedded to provide a rigid, interconnected scaffold that resists crack propagation and plastic deformation, ensuring long-term durability. The adapted solution proposes using rigid, nanoscale Metal-Organic Polyhedra (MOPs) as a dispersed 'molecular rebar' scaffold within the polymer matrix. The MOPs' robust coordination bonds and cage structure are intended to physically hinder polymer chain relaxation and segmental mobility—the primary mechanisms of physical aging—while potentially introducing beneficial, stable free volume. This approach aims to impart long-term stability against aging, preserving the membrane's separation performance by creating a composite with a reinforced, less-aging-prone microstructure.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Mapping of Concentration-Dependent Transport Landscapes in Polymer Libraries
ideas.description: This research direction integrates robotic polymer synthesis and high-throughput permeability testing with advanced, non-constant diffusion modeling. The automated platform will generate dense, multi-point permeability and sorption datasets across a wide range of penetrant activities for each polymer in a vast library. This high-dimensional data is then used to fit and validate concentration-dependent diffusion models (e.g., based on free-volume or thermodynamic frameworks) for thousands of candidate materials simultaneously. The synergy enables the rapid construction of 'transport mechanism maps' that correlate specific polymer chemical motifs with their dominant nonlinear diffusion behaviors, accelerating the discovery of materials tailored for applications where performance depends on variable feed conditions, such as gas separations or drug delivery.

ideas.title: A generative, physics-informed model for membrane transport property prediction trained on unified solubility-diffusivity data
ideas.description: This project develops a predictive platform that moves beyond a static database by using compiled solubility and diffusivity data to train a generative machine learning model. The model explicitly accounts for chemical structure, processing conditions, and membrane morphology, incorporating physical constraints from transport theory to ensure robustness. It enables the inverse design of membrane materials by predicting the necessary chemical features and processing pathways to achieve desired permeability and selectivity targets for specific gas or liquid separations.

ideas.title: Roll-to-Roll Manufacturing with In-Line Molecular Dynamics Feedback for Adaptive Membrane Synthesis
ideas.description: This research direction integrates molecular dynamics (MD) simulations directly into a continuous roll-to-roll manufacturing line for large-area polymer membranes. In-line sensors monitor key material states (e.g., polymer chain alignment, solvent content) during fabrication, and this data seeds rapid, targeted MD simulations that predict the evolving penetrant diffusion pathways in the forming film. The simulation outputs then provide real-time feedback to dynamically adjust downstream processing parameters—such as tension, temperature, or coagulation bath conditions—to actively steer the polymer matrix toward a desired non-equilibrium microstructure. This synergy enables the scalable production of membranes with intentionally optimized and heterogeneous transport pathways, moving beyond static membrane design to a dynamic, simulation-guided synthesis paradigm.

ideas.title: Synthesize polymers with 'molecular knots' inspired by knotted proteins to create rigid, permanent microporosity
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural biology—the use of knotted topologies to create ultra-stable, rigid protein structures—to the challenge of designing polymers with contorted backbones for size-sieving membranes. In nature, knotted proteins achieve their rigidity through a folded topology that cannot be undone without unfolding the entire chain. The analogous solution is to synthetically engineer this topological constraint into a polymer backbone. The adapted research idea is to synthesize polymers with 'molecular knots' by designing monomers that, upon polymerization, force the chain into a predetermined, knotted configuration with persistent loops and crossings. This creates a backbone with permanent, intrinsic microporosity and extreme rigidity, directly inhibiting chain packing and leading to highly selective, stable molecular sieves for gas separation or filtration.

ideas.title: Apply Fluorescence Correlation Spectroscopy (FCS) from biophysics to map nanoscale diffusivity and confinement in membrane free volume
ideas.description: This idea adapts Fluorescence Correlation Spectroscopy (FCS), a technique from biophysics used to study molecular diffusion and local confinement within cellular nano-environments like membranes and organelles, to the study of synthetic polymeric membranes. The analogous solution uses the analysis of fluorescence intensity fluctuations from single molecules traversing a tiny confocal volume to extract diffusion coefficients and local viscosity. Adapted to materials science, a fluorescent molecular probe compatible with the polymer would be introduced. By performing FCS measurements at different depths within the membrane, one can characterize the nanoscale diffusivity, effective pore size, and tortuosity of the free volume network in operational conditions (e.g., under humidity, pressure, or solvent exposure), offering a dynamic, spatially resolved complement to the positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy method.

ideas.title: Closed-loop multi-property optimization of polymer membranes via active learning-driven robotic screening
ideas.description: This redesigned system employs robotic synthesis and high-throughput testing to measure not just permeability, but also selectivity and key stability metrics for each polymer candidate. An active learning algorithm, trained on this multi-faceted data, models the complex property landscape and proposes the next most informative set of polymers to synthesize, targeting optimal trade-offs. This creates a closed-loop discovery pipeline that efficiently converges on high-performance membrane materials with balanced properties for specific gas or liquid separations, moving beyond simple high-throughput screening to intelligent, goal-directed exploration.

ideas.title: The 'Good Enough' Filler: A Pareto Frontier Analysis for MOFs vs. Inorganic Fillers
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that MOFs are the default best filler class by adapting a solution from pharmaceutical drug development. In that field, expensive new biologics are rigorously compared to cheaper, established small-molecule drugs using Pareto frontier analysis to justify their use in specific niches. The adapted idea applies this multi-criteria decision framework to filler materials. We will define a relevant performance-cost space encompassing key metrics like adsorption capacity, kinetics, chemical stability, unit cost, and ease of integration. By plotting both high-performance MOFs and simpler, cheaper inorganic fillers (e.g., zeolites, activated carbon, porous silica) on this frontier, we can move beyond blanket statements. The outcome will be a quantitative map identifying the precise application regimes (specific performance targets or environmental conditions) where the investment in MOFs is truly warranted, and, crucially, where conventional fillers represent the 'good enough' or even superior cost-performance choice.

ideas.title: Defect-tolerant, sub-100-nm selective membranes with integrated self-healing sub-layers
ideas.description: This approach fabricates ultrathin, high-permeance selective layers (sub-100-nm) on a novel hierarchical support engineered for defect tolerance. The key innovation is a functional, responsive sub-layer integrated between the support and the selective film, which contains mobile healing agents. When a defect forms in the primary selective layer, the applied pressure gradient triggers the targeted migration and sealing of the breach by these agents, restoring selectivity locally. This design maintains the theoretical permeance advantage of an ultrathin film while providing robust, long-term selectivity even in the presence of inevitable fabrication defects or operational damage.

ideas.title: Decoupling Solubility and Diffusivity via Bio-Inspired Gating Mechanisms
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that polymer modifications which increase solubility necessarily decrease diffusivity. Drawing an analogy from membrane biophysics, where ion channels decouple selectivity (solubility) from conductance (diffusivity), we propose to design block copolymers with architecturally separated functions. One polymer block will be tailored for high solute affinity and solubility, acting as a molecular recognition gate. A second, contiguous block will be engineered to be structurally rigid and hydrophobic, creating a low-friction pathway that promotes rapid solute diffusion. By adapting this bio-inspired gating mechanism, the research seeks a synergistic effect where both solubility and diffusivity are independently optimized within a single material.

ideas.description: This approach creates a robust, predictive platform by coupling the Sanchez-Lacombe equation-of-state with molecular simulation and machine learning. High-throughput molecular dynamics simulations provide initial solubility data for a diverse training set of polymer-penetrant systems under pressure. These data are used to calibrate the lattice fluid model's parameters and to train a machine learning model that predicts the crucial binary interaction parameters for novel, uncharacterized material pairs. The resulting hybrid framework significantly reduces the need for prior experimental data, enabling reliable solubility predictions for new high-pressure polymer applications like carbon capture or hydrogen storage with minimal calibration.

ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the averaging limitation of standalone PALS by creating a correlative, multi-scale characterization system. It combines positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) for ensemble-averaged sub-nanometer free volume data with high-resolution 3D X-ray or electron tomography to map the nanoscale morphology and connectivity of free volume elements. A dedicated analysis framework then correlates these datasets, allowing researchers to distinguish between isolated pores and interconnected transport pathways and to link specific structural features with positron lifetime components. This system provides a robust, spatially informed picture of free volume that directly connects to membrane transport models and performance.

ideas.title: Concentration-Dependent Anomalous Diffusion in Glassy Polymers: A Unified Framework for Strongly Interacting Systems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified theoretical and experimental framework to model penetrant diffusion in glassy polymers where the diffusion coefficient is both concentration-dependent and the transport mechanism is explicitly non-Fickian. The core synergy is investigating how strong penetrant-polymer interactions, which cause concentration dependence, simultaneously induce a transition from anomalous (sub-diffusive) to Fickian transport as concentration increases and the system approaches the glass transition. The work will integrate concentration-dependent diffusivity into fractional calculus or continuous-time random walk models to predict how sorption kinetics and penetrant clustering dynamically modify the polymer's free volume and relaxation timescales, offering a predictive tool for complex systems like water in epoxies or solvents in high-performance barriers.

ideas.title: Predictive Design of Engineered Non-Ideal Interfaces via Unified Transport Database
ideas.description: This research direction integrates a unified solubility-diffusivity database with the deliberate engineering of non-ideal interfaces. The database provides the foundational thermodynamic and kinetic parameters for penetrants in bulk phases, which are essential inputs for physics-based models that simulate transport across engineered, chemically incompatible interfaces. This enables the predictive design of such interfaces—such as controlled interphases, grafted layers, or mismatched polymer blends—to create novel, selective transport pathways that would be impossible in homogeneous materials. The resulting experimental data on these non-ideal systems then feeds back into the database, expanding its scope beyond ideal bulk materials to include validated parameters for engineered interfacial transport phenomena.

It embeds hard constraints for chemical stability and synthetic feasibility directly into the search algorithm, using known reaction databases and retrosynthetic analysis tools. Each candidate is then stress-tested in silico against a suite of secondary performance metrics critical for industrial application, such as plasticization resistance and mechanical durability. The final output is a ranked set of experimentally actionable designs, complete with predicted full-property profiles and synthetic pathways.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Polymer Membranes via Multi-Objective Generative Adversarial Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse design paradigm from computational drug discovery to polymer membrane engineering. In drug discovery, generative models are used to create novel molecules with predefined biological activities. Here, we propose training a multi-objective generative adversarial network (GAN) on a comprehensive polymer property database. The model will learn the complex relationship between polymer repeat unit structures (e.g., functional groups, chain flexibility, polarity) and their gas transport properties. Given a set of target permeabilities for specific gases, the trained model will perform a guided search in the chemical latent space to generate entirely new, synthetically plausible polymer structures optimized for the desired separation performance, effectively solving the inverse design problem for membrane materials.

ideas.title: Apply Bioadhesive Mussel-Inspired Chemistry to MOF-Polymer Interfaces
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from marine bioadhesion, where mussels use catechol-rich proteins to bind to diverse surfaces, this idea adapts mussel-inspired chemistry to improve MOF-polymer adhesion. The analogous solution involves synthetic replication of catechol chemistry via polydopamine or functional polymers. Adapted to MOF-polymer composites, the research idea is to synthesize or functionalize MOF particles with catechol-like ligands or apply a thin polydopamine coating to the MOF surface. This creates a reactive interface that can form strong physical and covalent bonds with a wide range of polymer matrices, dramatically improving interfacial adhesion and stress transfer in the composite, especially under humid conditions, thereby enhancing mechanical properties and durability.

ideas.title: Engineered Non-Ideal Interfaces with Controlled Defect Networks for Selective Transport
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that chemical compatibility at material interfaces is essential for optimal transport selectivity. Drawing an analogy from semiconductor physics, where engineered grain boundaries transformed from defects into functional components, we propose to deliberately create controlled, non-ideal chemical interfaces in separation or catalytic systems. The analogous solution involved mastering the density, orientation, and chemistry of grain boundaries in polycrystalline silicon to create beneficial electronic properties. Adapted to chemical transport, this means engineering interfaces with specific chemical mismatches, strain fields, or impurity phases to create novel, selective transport pathways. These engineered 'defect networks' would function not as perfect filters but as dynamic, interactive regions where selectivity emerges from the controlled non-ideality itself, potentially enabling new separation mechanisms beyond size-exclusion or simple chemical affinity.

ideas.description: This research direction combines continuous roll-to-roll (R2R) manufacturing with solution-diffusion membrane design to create a new paradigm in controlled release. The core innovation is the development of R2R-compatible processes, such as sequential slot-die coating, inkjet printing, or in-line UV grafting, to spatially pattern or laminate different polymer chemistries and loadings onto a single, continuous membrane web. This allows for the production of meter-scale membrane sheets that can be cut into individual devices, each containing distinct zones engineered for the delayed or sustained release of different drugs (e.g., an antibiotic, an anti-inflammatory, and a growth factor) according to a pre-defined treatment schedule. The synergy lies in using scalable manufacturing to solve a key limitation in advanced drug delivery—the affordable fabrication of complex, multi-phasic release systems for personalized wound care, combination therapies, or vaccination schedules from a single, integrated platform.

This data feeds back to adjust deposition, curing, or coating parameters to correct drifts and minor defects on-the-fly. The final output is not just a roll of membrane, but a digitally certified map that guides the cutting of the roll into high-performance, quality-assured modules with known and validated properties, ensuring yield and reliability at scale.

ideas.title: Universal Dual-Mode Sorption via a Statistical Mechanics Framework
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the dual-mode sorption model applies only to glassy polymers with known, discrete microvoids by drawing an analogy from statistical mechanics. In the field of disordered magnetic systems like spin glasses, universal models succeeded by shifting focus from specific microscopic defects to a statistical description of local environments. Adapting this solution, we propose a research program to reframe dual-mode sorption. The core adapted idea is to treat the glassy polymer matrix not in terms of specific voids but as a statistically disordered solid characterized by a distribution of local sorption site energies and capacities. This framework would derive the dual-mode sorption isotherm parameters from this underlying distribution, theoretically making the model a universal constitutive equation for gas sorption in any glassy polymer, regardless of the specific origin of its non-equilibrium state.

ideas.title: Enhance MOF-polymer compatibility via bio-inspired linker functionalization
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the problem of MOF-polymer incompatibility by drawing an analogy from biomaterials science, where the integration of synthetic implants with biological tissue is achieved through bio-recognition. The analogous solution involves functionalizing an inert synthetic surface with specific bioactive peptides (like RGD) that bind to natural cell receptors. Adapted to MOF research, this translates to developing novel MOF linker functionalization strategies where the organic linkers are synthetically tailored to present terminal chemical functionalities designed for specific, favorable interactions (e.g., covalent bonding, supramolecular recognition, or dynamic bonding) with the repeating units of a chosen polymer matrix. The goal is to move beyond general compatibility to create a designed chemical interface that actively promotes adhesion and compatibility, much like a bioactive coating promotes osseointegration.

ideas.title: Closed-loop bioresponsive membranes for adaptive drug delivery
ideas.description: This system moves beyond passive, diffusion-controlled release by creating an intelligent membrane capable of self-regulation. It incorporates embedded biosensors that continuously monitor the local physiological milieu and a tunable actuator layer that adjusts membrane porosity or swelling in real-time. This closed-loop feedback allows the delivery system to maintain a precise, pre-defined release profile even in the face of dynamic biological conditions that would degrade a conventional membrane's performance. The approach aims to achieve robust, personalized therapeutic dosing for complex, variable disease states.

ideas.title: Adapting the Variable Viscosity Framework from Non-Newtonian Fluid Mechanics to Polymer Diffusion
ideas.description: This idea challenges the fundamental assumption of a constant diffusion coefficient in polymer-penetrant systems by drawing an analogy from non-Newtonian fluid mechanics. In that field, the failure of the constant viscosity assumption for complex fluids was addressed by developing constitutive models (e.g., power-law, Carreau) where viscosity is a function of shear rate, which is then embedded into the governing flow equations. Adapted to diffusion, this approach involves creating a constitutive model for the diffusion coefficient as a function of local penetrant concentration—for instance, D(C) = D0 * f(C/C0), where f is a function derived from free-volume theory or experimental sorption kinetics. This concentration-dependent D(C) is then substituted into Fick's second law, creating a nonlinear diffusion equation that more accurately models systems with strong penetrant-polymer interactions, such as solvent-induced swelling or plasticization, requiring advanced numerical methods for solution.

ideas.title: Closed-loop synthesis of polymers with validated free volume distribution via integrated in-situ characterization
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop synthetic platform that integrates real-time, quantitative characterization of free volume element distribution directly into polymer synthesis and processing. The system uses in-situ scattering and spectroscopic probes (e.g., PALS, X-ray scattering) paired with computational forward models to provide immediate feedback on the nanoscale void architecture as it forms. This feedback directly controls synthetic parameters—such as monomer feed rates, cross-linking density, or thermal history—enabling iterative, model-informed tuning of the free volume distribution. The outcome is a robust methodology that not only creates polymers with targeted free volume but also provides continuous, validated proof of the achieved nanostructure.

ideas.title: Adapting Kinetic Monte Carlo from Catalysis to Model Penetrant Diffusion in Polymers
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from heterogeneous catalysis—Kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulation—to the problem of modeling penetrant diffusion in polymers. In catalysis, kMC is used to simulate long-timescale, thermally-activated surface processes by stochastically sampling predefined event rates derived from more detailed calculations. Adapted here, short molecular dynamics (MD) simulations will first be used to map the polymer's free-volume landscape and calculate transition rates for penetrant hopping between sites. These rates will then feed into a kMC simulation, which can efficiently model the stochastic sequence of hops over microseconds or longer, providing a complete picture of diffusion pathways and mechanisms that are inaccessible to direct MD alone.

ideas.title: Adapting Semiconductor Photolithography Quality Control for Continuous Membrane Manufacturing
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed on scaling up membrane module manufacturing by drawing an analogy from semiconductor photolithography, specifically the roll-to-roll manufacturing of flexible electronics. The analogous problem—maintaining nanoscale precision and uniformity across large, continuously moving substrates—was solved using integrated, in-line optical metrology (like laser interferometry) with closed-loop feedback control to adjust process parameters in real time. The adapted research idea is to develop and integrate a similar suite of non-contact, real-time sensors and control systems into a continuous roll-to-roll membrane production line. These systems would monitor critical performance parameters (e.g., selective layer thickness, pore size distribution, defect density) and use the data to automatically adjust preceding process steps—such as polymer solution coating, solvent exchange, or curing—ensuring the consistent manufacture of large-area, high-performance membrane sheets without halting the production line.

ideas.title: Adapting Phage Display for Polymer Discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput screening logic of phage display—where a vast biological library is screened by linking function (phenotype) to an identifiable tag (genotype)—from biomedical engineering to polymer science. The analogous solution uses bacteriophages to display peptides, enabling iterative selection rounds (biopanning) to find candidates with high binding affinity. Adapted to the original research context, the method involves implementing robotic synthesis to create vast libraries of polymer candidates where each unique polymer is synthesized on or within a uniquely barcoded bead or droplet. This creates a direct phenotype (permeability, tested rapidly in parallel) to genotype (barcode) link. The entire library can be screened in a single batch using rapid permeability assays, and high-performing candidates are instantly identified by decoding their barcodes, dramatically accelerating the discovery cycle for advanced polymer membranes.

ideas.title: Topologically Encoded Metastable Interfaces for Pathway-Specific Transport
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply relaxing compatibility to actively designing interfaces with a specific, non-random architecture of defects. Computational materials informatics identifies metastable interfacial states where defect motifs create beneficial transport bottlenecks or resonance conditions for target species. Synthesis then uses kinetic control and templating agents to lock in these pre-selected topological patterns at the interface, creating reproducible 'designer disorder'. This yields interfaces that are chemically non-ideal but topologically precise, ensuring the novel transport pathways are dominant, predictable, and engineerable.
